# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a pendant vertex. Is it possible for a complete graph $K_n$ (with $n \geq 2$) to have a pendant vertex? Justify.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** A vertex $v$ in a graph $G$ is called a **pendant vertex** if $\deg(v) = 1$, i.e., it is incident to exactly one edge.
>
> **Justification (2 marks):** In the complete graph $K_n$, every vertex is adjacent to **all** other $n-1$ vertices. Therefore, $\deg(v) = n - 1$ for every vertex $v$. For a pendant vertex, we would require $\deg(v) = 1$, i.e., $n - 1 = 1$, giving $n = 2$. So $K_2$ is the only complete graph that has pendant vertices (both its vertices are pendant). For $n \geq 3$, $K_n$ has **no pendant vertex**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a null graph? How many edges does a null graph on $n$ vertices have? Mention one real-world analogy.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** A **null graph** on $n$ vertices, denoted $N_n$, is a simple graph with vertex set $V$ of cardinality $n$ and an **empty edge set** $E = \varnothing$. Equivalently, it is a graph with $n$ isolated vertices and no edges.
>
> **Edges (0.5 mark):** The number of edges in $N_n$ is exactly $0$.
>
> **Analogy (0.5 mark):** A room full of $n$ people with no one talking to anyone else — the people are present (vertices), but there are no interactions (edges).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions are **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. KTU provides an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both alternatives are given below for your practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
**Consider the graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ defined as follows:**
$$G_1: V_1 = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}, \quad E_1 = \{\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,3\}, \{2,4\}, \{4,5\}, \{5,6\}\}.$$
$$G_2: V_2 = \{a,b,c,d,e,f\}, \quad E_2 = \{\{a,c\}, \{a,d\}, \{c,d\}, \{c,b\}, \{b,e\}, \{e,f\}\}.$$
**Determine whether $G_1 \cong G_2$. If isomorphic, exhibit the bijection; otherwise, name the invariant that distinguishes them.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vertex and edge counts (1 mark):**

$$|V_1| = 6 = |V_2|, \qquad |E_1| = 6 = |E_2|.$$

**Step 2 — Degree sequences (2 marks):**

For $G_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(1) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{1,2\}, \{1,3\}), \\
\deg(2) &= 3 \ (\text{edges } \{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,4\}), \\
\deg(3) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{1,2\}, \{1,3\}), \\
\deg(4) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{2,4\}, \{4,5\}), \\
\deg(5) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{4,5\}, \{5,6\}), \\
\deg(6) &= 1 \ (\text{edge } \{5,6\}).
\end{aligned}
$$

Sorted: $(1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 3)$.

For $G_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(a) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{a,c\}, \{a,d\}), \\
\deg(b) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{c,b\}, \{b,e\}), \\
\deg(c) &= 3 \ (\text{edges } \{a,c\}, \{a,d\}, \{c,b\}), \\
\deg(d) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{a,d\}, \{c,d\}), \\
\deg(e) &= 2 \ (\text{edges } \{b,e\}, \{e,f\}), \\
\deg(f) &= 1 \ (\text{edge } \{e,f\}).
\end{aligned}
$$

Sorted: $(1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 3)$.

**Step 3 — Pendant vertex check (1 mark):** Both have exactly **1 pendant vertex** (vertex $6$ in $G_1$, vertex $f$ in $G_2$). Component count is 1 in both (both connected).

**Step 4 — Construct bijection (2 marks):** Match degree-3 to degree-3, degree-1 to degree-1, then fill in the rest. Try:

$$f(1) = d, \ f(2) = c, \ f(3) = a, \ f(4) = b, \ f(5) = e, \ f(6) = f.$$

**Step 5 — Verify edge preservation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{1,2\} &\mapsto \{d,c\} = \{c,d\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark \\
\{1,3\} &\mapsto \{d,a\} = \{a,d\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark \\
\{2,3\} &\mapsto \{c,a\} = \{a,c\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark \\
\{2,4\} &\mapsto \{c,b\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark \\
\{4,5\} &\mapsto \{b,e\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark \\
\{5,6\} &\mapsto \{e,f\} \in E_2 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All edges map to edges, and the bijection is one-to-one and onto. **Therefore $G_1 \cong G_2$ with the isomorphism $f$ given above. (7/7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
**State and prove that every non-trivial tree has at least two pendant vertices.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Let $T$ be a tree (connected acyclic graph) with at least one edge. Then $T$ has **at least two pendant vertices**.

**Proof (6 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** A tree is connected, so there exists a path between any two vertices. In particular, since $T$ has at least one edge, pick any edge $\{u, v\}$ — there is a path of length 1 between them.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Consider a **longest path** $P$ in $T$, say $P: x_0 - x_1 - x_2 - \dots - x_k$ (with $k \geq 1$ since $T$ has $\geq 1$ edge). By *maximality* of $P$, no edge can be extended from either endpoint.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Look at the endpoint $x_0$. The vertex $x_0$ is connected to $x_1$ via the path. Suppose, for contradiction, that $x_0$ has a **second neighbor** $y \neq x_1$. Then $y$ must lie on $P$ (otherwise $y - x_0 - x_1 - \dots - x_k$ would be a longer path, contradicting maximality of $P$). But if $y = x_i$ for some $i \geq 1$, then the cycle $y - x_0 - x_1 - \dots - x_i$ exists, **contradicting the acyclicity** of $T$. Hence $x_0$ has only the neighbor $x_1$, i.e., $\deg(x_0) = 1$.

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** By symmetric reasoning applied to the other endpoint $x_k$, we get $\deg(x_k) = 1$. Since $x_0 \neq x_k$ (the path has length $\geq 1$), we have found **two distinct pendant vertices**.

- **Conclusion:** Every non-trivial tree has at least two pendant vertices. ∎ **(7/7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students **incorrectly argue** that pendant vertices must be even in number (this is true *only* in *caterpillar trees* and certain other special classes — not in general trees). For the "at least 2 leaves" theorem, do **NOT** use any parity argument. Stick to the longest-path argument above — that is the standard KTU-board-accepted proof.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Show that the following two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ are not isomorphic.**

$$G_1: V_1 = \{1,2,3,4\}, \quad E_1 = \{\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{1,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{2,4\}, \{3,4\}\} \quad (\text{this is } K_4).$$
$$G_2: V_2 = \{a,b,c,d\}, \quad E_2 = \{\{a,b\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,d\}, \{d,a\}, \{a,c\}\} \quad (\text{this is } C_4 \text{ plus diagonal}).$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vertex and edge counts (1 mark):**

$$|V_1| = 4 = |V_2|, \qquad |E_1| = 6 \neq 5 = |E_2|.$$

**Step 2 — Apply the edge-count invariant (3 marks):**

The number of edges is a graph invariant preserved by isomorphism. Since $|E_1| = 6$ and $|E_2| = 5$, the counts differ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differing edge counts alone are sufficient** to conclude that $G_1 \not\cong G_2$. (3 marks)

**Step 3 — Confirm with degree sequences (2 marks):**

For $G_1 = K_4$: every vertex has degree $3$. Sorted: $(3, 3, 3, 3)$.

For $G_2 = C_4 + \text{diagonal}$: vertices $a$ and $c$ have degree $3$, while $b$ and $d$ have degree $2$. Sorted: $(2, 2, 3, 3)$.

**Step 4 — Final conclusion (1 mark):**

Both invariants (edge count and degree sequence) fail to match, so:

$$\boxed{G_1 \not\cong G_2} \quad \textbf{(7/7 marks)}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define a null graph. Prove that the null graph $N_n$ on $n$ vertices is bipartite, and find its chromatic number.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A null graph $N_n$ is a simple graph with vertex set $V$ of size $n$ and empty edge set $E = \varnothing$.

**Bipartiteness proof (4 marks):**

- A graph is **bipartite** if its vertex set can be partitioned into two subsets $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that every edge has one endpoint in $V_1$ and the other in $V_2$.

- For $N_n$, we can choose **any** partition, e.g., $V_1 = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}\}$ and $V_2 = \{v_{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1}, \dots, v_n\}$.

- Since $E = \varnothing$, the condition "every edge has one endpoint in each subset" is **vacuously true** — there are no edges to violate the condition.

- Therefore, $N_n$ is bipartite. ∎

**Chromatic number (2 marks):**

The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ is the minimum number of colors needed to color the vertices such that no two adjacent vertices share a color. In $N_n$, since there are no edges, **no two vertices are adjacent**. Hence, all $n$ vertices can be colored with the **same single color** without conflict.

$$\chi(N_n) = 1.$$

In fact, $\chi(N_n) = 1$ for all $n \geq 1$, since $N_n$ is an *independent set* (no edges ⇒ trivial coloring with 1 color).

**(7/7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on Isomorphism Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT** claim two graphs are isomorphic just because their degree sequences match — this is a *necessary*, not *sufficient* condition. Always verify with an explicit bijection.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to also check that **non-edges** map to non-edges under the bijection. KTU examiners often award a partial mark for showing the bijection preserves edges but deduct for missing the non-edge check.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse the **null graph** $N_n$ (no edges, $n$ vertices) with the **empty graph** $\varnothing$ (no vertices, no edges). They are *not* the same object.
> 4. **Do NOT** use the "even number of pendant vertices" claim on general graphs — only certain classes satisfy this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Focus**

- ⭐ **Pendant Vertex**: A vertex $v$ with $\deg(v) = 1$. The single incident edge is the *pendant edge*.

- ⭐ **Pendant Vertex Existence**: Trees with $\geq 1$ edge must have $\geq 2$ pendant vertices. $K_n$ for $n \geq 3$ has none. Cycles $C_n$ for $n \geq 3$ have none.

- ⭐ **Null Graph $N_n$**: $n$ vertices, $0$ edges. All vertices isolated. Component count = $n$. $\chi(N_n) = 1$. It is bipartite, an *edgeless* graph, and an *independent set*.

- ⭐ **Isomorphism — Necessary Conditions** (must all match for any chance of isomorphism):
  1. Number of vertices
  2. Number of edges
  3. Degree sequence (sorted)
  4. Number of connected components
  5. Number of pendant vertices, number of isolated vertices
  6. Cycle counts of each length, girth
  7. Diameter, chromatic number

- ⭐ **Isomorphism — Sufficient Condition**: An *explicit bijection* $f: V_1 \to V_2$ such that $\{u,v\} \in E_1 \iff \{f(u), f(v)\} \in E_2$.

- ⭐ **Isomorphism is an equivalence relation**: Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive.

- ⭐ **Standard Isomorphism Proof Strategy** (in KTU exams):
  - Show all invariants match.
  - Construct a candidate bijection (usually by matching vertices of equal degree).
  - Verify *all* edges and non-edges are preserved.
  - Conclude $G_1 \cong G_2$.

- ⭐ **Standard Non-Isomorphism Proof Strategy**:
  - Find **one** invariant that differs between the two graphs.
  - State the invariant, compare the values, and conclude $G_1 \not\cong G_2$.

- ⭐ **Handshaking Lemma**: $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2|E|$ — always holds, useful for sanity checks.

- ⭐ **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Confusing *null graph* $N_n$ with *trivially empty graph* $\varnothing$. The former has $n$ isolated vertices; the latter has none.

- ⭐ **Engineering Applications**: Chemical isomer detection (drug design), network topology equivalence, AST leaf-node analysis (compiler design), social network structure comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subgraphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science  4
> - **Module:** Module  1
> - **Topic:** Subgraphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Subgraph

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a graph with vertex set $V$ and edge set $E$. A graph $H = (V', E')$ is called a **subgraph** of $G$, written as $H \subseteq G$, if and only if:

$$V'(H) \subseteq V(G) \quad \text{and} \quad E'(H) \subseteq E(G)$$

with the additional condition that every edge in $E'(H)$ has **both of its endpoints** in $V'(H)$. In other words, an edge cannot exist in $H$ unless both of its incident vertices are also present in $H$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected in exams):**
> *"A subgraph of a graph $G$ is a graph whose vertex set and edge set are subsets of the vertex set and edge set of $G$ respectively."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **subgraph** as a *zoomed-in photograph* of a complex social network. Imagine the full social network is the whole graph $G$ (e.g., a university with 10,000 students and 50,000 friendships). Now zoom into just the **Computer Science Department** — you see only those 200 students (a subset of vertices) and only the friendships among them (a subset of edges). That smaller, focused network is your subgraph.

**Another analogy:** Consider a city's complete metro map. A *subgraph* is any one specific metro line (say, the Red Line) showing only those stations and the track segments connecting them, even though the tracks continue further in the real system.

> [!NOTE]
> The two conditions of a subgraph are:
> 1. **Vertex subset condition:** $V' \subseteq V$
> 2. **Edge subset condition:** $E' \subseteq E$
> 3. **Incidence condition:** If $e = (u, v) \in E'$, then $u, v \in V'$
>
> A *single isolated vertex* (with no edges) is always a valid subgraph of any graph containing that vertex.

## 1.3 Why Subgraphs Matter in Computer Science

Subgraphs are the foundational building block for many real-world CS problems:

- **Network Analysis:** Finding communities or clusters in a social network.
- **Database Querying:** SQL JOIN operations correspond to subgraph operations.
- **Circuit Design:** Validating sub-circuits before integration.
- **Compiler Design:** Control flow graphs are analyzed as subgraphs of program execution graphs.
- **Map Routing:** Subgraphs are extracted for regions to compute shortest paths efficiently.

**Standard metrics used in this topic:**

- **Order** of a graph $G$ (denoted $|V(G)|$): the number of vertices
- **Size** of a graph $G$ (denoted $|E(G)|$): the number of edges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examination will test your ability to *distinguish* between the various types of subgraphs (proper, spanning, induced). Marks are frequently lost when students confuse **induced subgraphs** with arbitrary subgraphs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Subgraphs

A subgraph $H$ of $G$ can be classified into one of several types based on how its vertex and edge sets relate to those of $G$:

### A. Spanning Subgraph
A subgraph $H$ of $G$ is called a **spanning subgraph** if it contains **all the vertices** of $G$, i.e.:

$$V(H) = V(G) \quad \text{and} \quad E(H) \subseteq E(G)$$

The edge set may be any subset, including the empty set. The graph $G$ itself and the graph containing all vertices of $G$ with no edges are both spanning subgraphs.

### B. Proper Subgraph
A subgraph $H$ of $G$ is called a **proper subgraph** if $H \neq G$. This means either $V(H) \subsetneq V(G)$ or $E(H) \subsetneq E(G)$ (or both).

### C. Induced Subgraph (Vertex-Induced Subgraph)
Given a subset $S \subseteq V(G)$, the **subgraph induced by $S$** (denoted $G[S]$ or $\langle S \rangle$) is the subgraph obtained by:

- Taking $S$ as the vertex set, **and**
- Including **every edge** of $G$ that has **both endpoints in $S$**.

Formally:

$$G[S] = (S, \, E')$$

where

$$E' = \{ e = (u,v) \in E(G) \mid u \in S \text{ and } v \in S \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an induced subgraph, the edge set is **maximized** for the chosen vertex set — every "possible" edge between chosen vertices is present.

### D. Edge-Induced Subgraph
Given a subset $F \subseteq E(G)$, the **subgraph induced by $F$** (denoted $G[F]$) is the subgraph whose:

- Vertex set consists of **all endpoints** of edges in $F$, and
- Edge set is exactly $F$.

$$G[F] = (V', F), \quad \text{where } V' = \{ u, v \in V(G) \mid (u,v) \in F \}$$

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Subgraph Types

The relationships between types of subgraphs can be summarized as follows:

1. Every spanning subgraph is a subgraph.
2. Every induced subgraph is a subgraph.
3. Every edge-induced subgraph is a subgraph.
4. Proper subgraphs include all of the above except the graph itself.
5. The **intersection** of two subgraphs of $G$ is also a subgraph of $G$.
6. The **union** of two subgraphs of $G$ (with shared vertices) is also a subgraph of $G$.

> [!NOTE]
> A subgraph of $G$ that is **both spanning and induced** is precisely $G$ itself.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Subgraph relation | $H \subseteq G$ | $V(H) \subseteq V(G)$ and $E(H) \subseteq E(G)$ |
| Proper subgraph | $H \subsetneq G$ | $H$ is a subgraph and $H \neq G$ |
| Spanning subgraph | $V(H) = V(G)$ | All vertices retained, any subset of edges |
| Vertex-induced subgraph | $G[S]$, $S \subseteq V(G)$ | $V' = S$, includes all $G$-edges within $S$ |
| Edge-induced subgraph | $G[F]$, $F \subseteq E(G)$ | $V' = $ endpoints of $F$, $E' = F$ |
| Order of $G$ | $\vert V(G) \vert$ | Number of vertices in $G$ |
| Size of $G$ | $\vert E(G) \vert$ | Number of edges in $G$ |
| Number of subgraphs | $\sum_{k=1}^{n}\binom{n}{k} \cdot 2^{\binom{k}{2}}$ | For simple graph of order $n$ (vertex sets only) |
| Max edges in $G[S]$ | $\binom{\vert S \vert}{2}$ | For simple graph, $\vert S \vert$ vertices |

## 2.4 Counting Subgraphs of a Simple Graph

For a simple graph $G$ of order $n$, the number of distinct subgraphs (considering vertex subsets with all possible edge subsets within them) is given by:

$$N_{\text{sub}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \cdot 2^{\binom{k}{2}}$$

This is because:
- Choose a non-empty vertex subset of size $k$ in $\binom{n}{k}$ ways.
- For each such subset, the maximum number of possible edges within it is $\binom{k}{2}$.
- Each edge can independently be present or absent, giving $2^{\binom{k}{2}}$ choices.

> [!NOTE]
> If we exclude the trivial single-vertex case, the formula begins at $k=1$. If we also include the empty graph, we would add 1 to this count.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Graph databases (Neo4j):** Queries like "find all people within 2 hops" are subgraph extraction operations.
- **Network reliability:** The probability that a network remains connected is computed by summing over all spanning subgraphs.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein-protein interaction networks are studied by extracting subgraphs corresponding to specific biological pathways.
- **VLSI Design:** Circuit verification involves checking whether a candidate circuit is a valid subgraph of the design specification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Identifying Induced Subgraphs

**Problem:** Consider the graph $G$ with vertex set $V(G) = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and edge set

$$E(G) = \{ab, bc, cd, de, ae, ac\}$$

Find the subgraph $G[S]$ induced by the vertex set $S = \{a, c, e\}$.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the vertex set of the induced subgraph:**

By definition of a vertex-induced subgraph, the vertex set is $S$ itself.

$$V(G[S]) = S = \{a, c, e\}$$

**Step 2 — Identify all edges in $E(G)$ that have BOTH endpoints in $S$:**

We inspect every edge in $E(G)$ one by one:

$$\begin{aligned}
&ab : a \in S, \, b \notin S \quad \Rightarrow \text{excluded} \\
&bc : b \notin S, \, c \in S \quad \Rightarrow \text{excluded} \\
&cd : c \in S, \, d \notin S \quad \Rightarrow \text{excluded} \\
&de : d \notin S, \, e \in S \quad \Rightarrow \text{excluded} \\
&ae : a \in S, \, e \in S \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{INCLUDED} \\
&ac : a \in S, \, c \in S \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{INCLUDED}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the induced subgraph:**

The induced subgraph $G[S]$ has the following components:

$$V(G[S]) = \{a, c, e\}, \quad E(G[S]) = \{ac, ae\}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

This is a valid subgraph of $G$ because:
- Every vertex of $G[S]$ is a vertex of $G$ ✓
- Every edge of $G[S]$ is an edge of $G$ ✓
- Every edge in $G[S]$ has both its endpoints in $V(G[S])$ ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Distinguishing Spanning vs. Proper vs. Induced

**Problem:** Given the cycle graph $C_5$ on vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with edges $\{12, 23, 34, 45, 15\}$, consider the following graphs and classify each:

- $H_1$: vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, edges $\{12, 23, 34\}$
- $H_2$: vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$, edges $\{12, 23\}$
- $H_3$: vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$, edges $\{12, 23, 13\}$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Analyze $H_1$:**

$$V(H_1) = \{1,2,3,4,5\} = V(C_5), \quad E(H_1) = \{12, 23, 34\} \subsetneq E(C_5)$$

Since $H_1$ contains all vertices of $C_5$ but only a strict subset of edges, $H_1$ is a **spanning subgraph** of $C_5$. It is also a **proper subgraph** because $H_1 \neq C_5$.

**Step 2 — Analyze $H_2$:**

$$V(H_2) = \{1,2,3\} \subsetneq V(C_5), \quad E(H_2) = \{12, 23\}$$

Since $H_2$ is missing vertex 4, it cannot contain the edges $34$ or $45$ or $15$ incident to 4. The edge set $\{12, 23\}$ is valid (both endpoints are in $V(H_2)$). So $H_2$ is a subgraph. It is **proper** (fewer vertices) but **NOT spanning** and **NOT induced** (the edge $13$ from $C_5$ is missing even though both 1 and 3 are present).

**Step 3 — Analyze $H_3$:**

$$V(H_3) = \{1,2,3\}, \quad E(H_3) = \{12, 23, 13\}$$

Wait — is the edge $13$ actually in $C_5$? The edges of $C_5$ are $\{12, 23, 34, 45, 15\}$. The edge $13$ is **not** in $C_5$. Therefore $H_3$ is **not a subgraph of $C_5$** at all, because the edge $13 \notin E(C_5)$.

**Correction/Refinement:** To make $H_3$ a valid subgraph, we would need to use only edges from $E(C_5)$. The vertex-induced subgraph of $C_5$ on $\{1,2,3\}$ would be:

$$C_5[\{1,2,3\}] = (\{1,2,3\}, \{12, 23\})$$

because $13 \notin E(C_5)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Counting Subgraphs

**Problem:** How many subgraphs does the path graph $P_3$ have? $P_3$ has vertex set $\{1, 2, 3\}$ and edge set $\{12, 23\}$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — List all possible non-empty vertex subsets:**

There are $2^3 - 1 = 7$ non-empty vertex subsets.

**Step 2 — For each vertex subset, count the possible subgraphs:**

For each non-empty $S \subseteq V(P_3)$, the number of valid subgraphs is $2^{e_S}$ where $e_S$ is the number of edges in $P_3$ with both endpoints in $S$.

| $S$ | Edges in $P_3$ within $S$ | $e_S$ | Subgraphs on $S$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\{1\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 0 | 1 |
| $\{2\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 0 | 1 |
| $\{3\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 0 | 1 |
| $\{1,2\}$ | $\{12\}$ | 1 | 2 |
| $\{1,3\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 0 | 1 |
| $\{2,3\}$ | $\{23\}$ | 1 | 2 |
| $\{1,2,3\}$ | $\{12, 23\}$ | 2 | 4 |

**Step 3 — Sum the counts:**

$$N_{\text{sub}} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 4 = 12$$

> [!NOTE]
> Adding the empty graph gives a total of **13 graphs** (including the empty graph) that are subgraphs of $P_3$. This matches the formula:
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{3}\binom{3}{k} 2^{\binom{k}{2}} = 1 \cdot 2^{0} + 3 \cdot 2^{1} + 1 \cdot 2^{3} = 1 + 6 + 8 = 15 \ldots$$
> (Note: this counts labeled subgraphs, including those on non-induced vertex sets. The 12 we computed counts only distinct subgraph structures, not labels.)

## 3.4 Theorem — Every Graph is a Subgraph of Itself

**Statement:** For any graph $G$, we have $G \subseteq G$ (reflexivity).

**Proof:**

We must show that $V(G) \subseteq V(G)$ and $E(G) \subseteq E(G)$, which is trivially true by set-theoretic reflexivity of subset inclusion ($A \subseteq A$ for any set $A$). $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Theorem — Subgraph Relation is Transitive

**Statement:** If $H \subseteq G$ and $G \subseteq K$, then $H \subseteq K$.

**Proof:**

Given: $V(H) \subseteq V(G)$, $E(H) \subseteq E(G)$, $V(G) \subseteq V(K)$, $E(G) \subseteq E(K)$.

By transitivity of set inclusion:

$$\begin{aligned}
V(H) &\subseteq V(G) \subseteq V(K) \Rightarrow V(H) \subseteq V(K) \\
E(H) &\subseteq E(G) \subseteq E(K) \Rightarrow E(H) \subseteq E(K)
\end{aligned}$$

Additionally, every edge in $E(H)$ has its endpoints in $V(H) \subseteq V(K)$, so the incidence condition holds. Therefore $H \subseteq K$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Graph and its Subgraphs (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows a parent graph $G$ and three different types of subgraphs extracted from it. The block-level representation is used because Mermaid's native graph syntax can be cluttered when many subgraphs are illustrated simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph parentG["Parent Graph G"]
        direction LR
        v1((V1)) --- v2((V2))
        v2 --- v3((V3))
        v3 --- v4((V4))
        v4 --- v5((V5))
        v1 --- v5
        v2 --- v4
    end

    subgraph subgraphA["Subgraph H1 Spanning"]
        direction LR
        h1a((V1)) --- h1b((V2))
        h1b --- h1c((V3))
        h1c --- h1d((V4))
        h1d --- h1e((V5))
    end

    subgraph subgraphB["Subgraph H2 Induced on V1 V3 V5"]
        direction LR
        h2a((V1)) --- h2b((V3))
        h2b --- h2c((V5))
        h2a --- h2c
    end

    subgraph subgraphC["Subgraph H3 Edge Induced on E12 E23"]
        direction LR
        h3a((V1)) --- h3b((V2))
        h3b --- h3c((V3))
    end

    parentG --> subgraphA
    parentG --> subgraphB
    parentG --> subgraphC

    style parentG fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style subgraphA fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style subgraphB fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style subgraphC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart for Subgraphs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Given a graph H and parent graph G"] --> checkV{"Is V H a subset of V G?"}
    checkV -- No --> rejectA["NOT a subgraph of G"]
    checkV -- Yes --> checkE{"Is E H a subset of E G?"}
    checkE -- No --> rejectB["NOT a subgraph of G"]
    checkE -- Yes --> checkInc{"Does every edge in E H have both endpoints in V H?"}
    checkInc -- No --> rejectC["NOT a subgraph of G"]
    checkInc -- Yes --> isSub["H is a valid subgraph of G"]

    isSub --> classQ{"Is H equal to G?"}
    classQ -- Yes --> isSelf["G is a subgraph of itself Reflexive case"]
    classQ -- No --> isProper["H is a PROPER subgraph"]

    isProper --> spanQ{"Does V H equal V G?"}
    spanQ -- Yes --> isSpan["H is a SPANNING subgraph of G"]
    spanQ -- No --> inducedQ{"Does E H contain ALL G-edges within V H?"}
    inducedQ -- Yes --> isInduced["H is a VERTEX INDUCED subgraph G S"]
    inducedQ -- No --> generic["H is a generic PROPER subgraph"]

    style startA fill:#bbdefb
    style isSub fill:#c8e6c9
    style isSelf fill:#fff9c4
    style isSpan fill:#ffe0b2
    style isInduced fill:#f8bbd0
    style generic fill:#d1c4e9
    style rejectA fill:#ffcdd2
    style rejectB fill:#ffcdd2
    style rejectC fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of Subgraph Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Vertex Set V of G"] --> opSelect{"Operation Type"}
    inputB["Edge Set E of G"] --> opSelect
    targetV["Target Vertex Set S subset V"] --> opSelect
    targetE["Target Edge Set F subset E"] --> opSelect

    opSelect -- "Choose S, keep all internal edges" --> inducedV["Vertex Induced Subgraph G S"]
    opSelect -- "Choose F, gather all endpoints" --> inducedE["Edge Induced Subgraph G F"]
    opSelect -- "Choose S and arbitrary F within" --> arbitrary["Arbitrary Subgraph H"]
    opSelect -- "Choose S equals V G, any F" --> span["Spanning Subgraph"]

    inducedV --> outA["Output: H with maximal edges for chosen vertices"]
    inducedE --> outB["Output: H with minimal vertices for chosen edges"]
    arbitrary --> outC["Output: H with both vertex and edge freedom"]
    span --> outD["Output: H with full vertex set"]

    style opSelect fill:#fff59d
    style inducedV fill:#b3e5fc
    style inducedE fill:#c5cae9
    style arbitrary fill:#dcedc8
    style span fill:#ffccbc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a subgraph. Differentiate between a spanning subgraph and an induced subgraph with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

A graph $H = (V', E')$ is called a **subgraph** of $G = (V, E)$ if $V' \subseteq V$ and $E' \subseteq E$ and every edge of $E'$ has both its endpoints in $V'$.

**Spanning Subgraph:** A subgraph $H$ of $G$ is spanning if $V(H) = V(G)$. *Example:* If $G = K_4$ with all 6 edges, then the cycle $C_4$ using only 4 of the 6 edges is a spanning subgraph.

**Induced Subgraph:** A subgraph $H$ of $G$ induced by $S \subseteq V(G)$ is denoted $G[S]$ and contains all edges of $G$ that lie entirely within $S$. *Example:* In $K_4$ on $\{1,2,3,4\}$, the subgraph $G[\{1,2,3\}]$ is a triangle $K_3$ containing all 3 possible edges.

**Key Difference:** A spanning subgraph preserves all vertices but may drop edges; an induced subgraph preserves all edges among the chosen vertices but may drop vertices.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is a vertex-induced subgraph? For the graph $G$ with $V = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and $E = \{ab, bc, cd, de, ae, ac, bd\}$, find $G[\{a, b, d\}]$.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A vertex-induced subgraph $G[S]$ of $G$ is the subgraph obtained by taking a vertex subset $S \subseteq V(G)$ and including all edges of $G$ that have both endpoints in $S$.

**Solution:**

Given $S = \{a, b, d\}$, the induced subgraph $G[\{a,b,d\}]$ has:
- $V(G[S]) = \{a, b, d\}$
- Edges with both endpoints in $S$: from $E(G)$, only $ab$ and $bd$ qualify.
- Edge $ae$: $e \notin S$, excluded.
- Edge $ac$: $c \notin S$, excluded.
- Edges $bc$, $cd$, $de$: have at least one endpoint outside $S$, excluded.

Therefore:

$$G[\{a, b, d\}] = (\{a, b, d\}, \{ab, bd\})$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Define a subgraph. Prove that the subgraph relation is reflexive and transitive.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (recap):** $H$ is a subgraph of $G$ if $V(H) \subseteq V(G)$, $E(H) \subseteq E(G)$, and every edge of $E(H)$ has both endpoints in $V(H)$.

**Reflexivity:** We must show $G \subseteq G$ for any graph $G$.

*Proof:* By the reflexivity of set inclusion, $V(G) \subseteq V(G)$ and $E(G) \subseteq E(G)$. The incidence condition holds trivially. Hence $G \subseteq G$. $\blacksquare$

**Transitivity:** We must show that if $H \subseteq G$ and $G \subseteq K$, then $H \subseteq K$.

*Proof:* Suppose $H \subseteq G$ and $G \subseteq K$.

By definition:

$$V(H) \subseteq V(G), \quad E(H) \subseteq E(G)$$

$$V(G) \subseteq V(K), \quad E(G) \subseteq E(K)$$

By transitivity of subset inclusion:

$$V(H) \subseteq V(G) \subseteq V(K) \Rightarrow V(H) \subseteq V(K)$$

$$E(H) \subseteq E(G) \subseteq E(K) \Rightarrow E(H) \subseteq E(K)$$

For any edge $e = (u, v) \in E(H)$: since $H$ is a subgraph of $G$, $u, v \in V(H)$. Since $V(H) \subseteq V(K)$, we have $u, v \in V(K)$. So every edge of $H$ has its endpoints in $V(K)$.

Therefore $H \subseteq K$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** For the graph $G$ shown below, with $V(G) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and $E(G) = \{12, 13, 24, 25, 35, 36, 46, 56\}$:

- (i) Find the subgraph $G[\{1, 2, 3, 5\}]$.
- (ii) Find the subgraph induced by the edge set $F = \{24, 35, 56\}$.
- (iii) Is the graph with vertex set $\{1, 2, 3, 5\}$ and edge set $\{12, 13, 25, 35\}$ a subgraph of $G$? Is it induced?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Vertex-induced subgraph $G[\{1, 2, 3, 5\}]$:**

- $V = \{1, 2, 3, 5\}$
- Edges in $E(G)$ with both endpoints in $\{1, 2, 3, 5\}$:
  - $12$: ✓ (both in set)
  - $13$: ✓ (both in set)
  - $24$: ✗ (4 not in set)
  - $25$: ✓ (both in set)
  - $35$: ✓ (both in set)
  - $36$: ✗ (6 not in set)
  - $46$: ✗
  - $56$: ✗

So:

$$G[\{1, 2, 3, 5\}] = (\{1, 2, 3, 5\}, \{12, 13, 25, 35\})$$

**(ii) Edge-induced subgraph $G[\{24, 35, 56\}]$:**

- Endpoints: $\{2, 4\} \cup \{3, 5\} \cup \{5, 6\} = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$
- Edges: $\{24, 35, 56\}$

So:

$$G[\{24, 35, 56\}] = (\{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}, \{24, 35, 56\})$$

**(iii) Analysis of $H = (\{1, 2, 3, 5\}, \{12, 13, 25, 35\})$:**

- Check vertex set: $\{1, 2, 3, 5\} \subseteq \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ ✓
- Check edge set: All four edges $\{12, 13, 25, 35\}$ are in $E(G)$ ✓
- Check incidence: All endpoints are in the chosen vertex set ✓
- **Therefore $H$ is a subgraph of $G$.**

Is it induced? Compare with $G[\{1, 2, 3, 5\}]$, which we found to have edges $\{12, 13, 25, 35\}$. The edge sets match, so **yes, $H$ is the induced subgraph** $G[\{1, 2, 3, 5\}]$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Define spanning subgraph, proper subgraph, and vertex-induced subgraph. How many subgraphs does $K_3$ (complete graph on 3 vertices) have?

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **Spanning Subgraph:** A subgraph $H$ of $G$ with $V(H) = V(G)$.
- **Proper Subgraph:** A subgraph $H$ of $G$ with $H \neq G$.
- **Vertex-Induced Subgraph:** A subgraph $G[S]$ for some $S \subseteq V(G)$ that contains all edges of $G$ with both endpoints in $S$.

**Counting subgraphs of $K_3$:**

$K_3$ has $V(K_3) = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $E(K_3) = \{12, 13, 23\}$ — three edges.

**Step 1 — Possible vertex subsets (excluding empty):**

- $\{1\}, \{2\}, \{3\}$ — 3 subsets of size 1
- $\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,3\}$ — 3 subsets of size 2
- $\{1,2,3\}$ — 1 subset of size 3

**Step 2 — For each vertex subset, enumerate all subgraphs:**

| $S$ | Internal edges in $K_3$ | Number of subgraphs on $S$ | List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\{1\}$ | none | 1 | just vertex 1 |
| $\{2\}$ | none | 1 | just vertex 2 |
| $\{3\}$ | none | 1 | just vertex 3 |
| $\{1,2\}$ | $\{12\}$ | 2 | isolated, edge $12$ |
| $\{1,3\}$ | $\{13\}$ | 2 | isolated, edge $13$ |
| $\{2,3\}$ | $\{23\}$ | 2 | isolated, edge $23$ |
| $\{1,2,3\}$ | $\{12, 13, 23\}$ | $2^3 = 8$ | 8 subgraphs on full vertex set |

**Step 3 — Total count:**

$$N = (1 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) + 8 = 3 + 6 + 8 = 17$$

Adding the empty graph: $17 + 1 = \mathbf{18}$ subgraphs of $K_3$ in total.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** State and prove that the intersection of two subgraphs of a graph $G$ is also a subgraph of $G$. What about their union?

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (Intersection):** If $H_1 = (V_1, E_1)$ and $H_2 = (V_2, E_2)$ are two subgraphs of $G$, then $H_1 \cap H_2 = (V_1 \cap V_2, \, E_1 \cap E_2)$ is also a subgraph of $G$.

**Proof:**

*Vertex set:* $V_1 \subseteq V(G)$ and $V_2 \subseteq V(G)$. By set intersection closure:

$$V_1 \cap V_2 \subseteq V(G)$$

*Edge set:* $E_1 \subseteq E(G)$ and $E_2 \subseteq E(G)$. Therefore:

$$E_1 \cap E_2 \subseteq E(G)$$

*Incidence condition:* Take any edge $e = (u, v) \in E_1 \cap E_2$. Then $e \in E_1$ and $e \in E_2$. Since $H_1$ is a subgraph, $u, v \in V_1$. Since $H_2$ is a subgraph, $u, v \in V_2$. Hence $u, v \in V_1 \cap V_2$.

All three subgraph conditions are satisfied, so $H_1 \cap H_2 \subseteq G$. $\blacksquare$

**Statement (Union):** If $H_1$ and $H_2$ are subgraphs of $G$, then $H_1 \cup H_2 = (V_1 \cup V_2, \, E_1 \cup E_2)$ is also a subgraph of $G$.

**Proof:**

*Vertex set:* $V_1 \cup V_2 \subseteq V(G) \cup V(G) = V(G)$. ✓

*Edge set:* $E_1 \cup E_2 \subseteq E(G) \cup E(G) = E(G)$. ✓

*Incidence condition:* For any $e = (u, v) \in E_1 \cup E_2$, either $e \in E_1$ (so $u, v \in V_1 \subseteq V_1 \cup V_2$) or $e \in E_2$ (so $u, v \in V_2 \subseteq V_1 \cup V_2$). In either case, $u, v \in V_1 \cup V_2$. ✓

Hence $H_1 \cup H_2$ is a subgraph of $G$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. When students compute induced subgraphs, they often **forget to include all relevant edges**. For example, with $S = \{a, b, c\}$ in a graph where $ab$, $bc$, AND $ac$ all exist, all three edges must be in $G[S]$ — not just some of them.
> 2. Students frequently **confuse "any subset" with "induced"**. A subgraph is induced ONLY if it contains every edge of $G$ between its vertices. If even one such edge is missing, the subgraph is *not* induced.
> 3. A common error: stating that the empty graph is a spanning subgraph. **It is not**, because spanning requires the vertex set to equal $V(G)$. The empty graph has an empty vertex set.
> 4. In counting problems, students forget to include the **empty graph** as a valid subgraph, or forget to enumerate single-vertex subgraphs separately.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Subgraph Definition:** A graph $H$ is a subgraph of $G$ if $V(H) \subseteq V(G)$, $E(H) \subseteq E(G)$, and every edge in $E(H)$ has both endpoints in $V(H)$.
- **Three conditions must hold** simultaneously for $H$ to qualify as a subgraph of $G$.
- **Spanning subgraph:** $V(H) = V(G)$ (all vertices retained).
- **Proper subgraph:** $H \neq G$ (at least one vertex or edge differs).
- **Vertex-induced subgraph $G[S]$:** Choose vertex set $S$; include **all** $G$-edges within $S$.
- **Edge-induced subgraph $G[F]$:** Choose edge set $F$; take **all endpoints** of $F$ as the vertex set.
- **Subgraph relation** is reflexive, transitive, and antisymmetric (forms a partial order on graphs).
- **Intersection and Union** of two subgraphs of $G$ are also subgraphs of $G$.
- **Empty graph** (no vertices, no edges) is a subgraph of every graph $G$.
- **A single isolated vertex** is always a valid subgraph of any graph containing it.
- **Counting formula** for subgraphs of a simple graph of order $n$ involves summing over all non-empty vertex subsets.
- **Real-world applications** include network analysis, database queries, bioinformatics, and VLSI verification.
- **Common exam traps:** confusing induced vs. arbitrary subgraphs; forgetting to check incidence condition; missing the empty graph in counting problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Walks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science  4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Walks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal language of **Graph Theory** (a foundational sub-discipline of Discrete Mathematics that underpins computer networks, database theory, and algorithm design), a **Walk** in an undirected (or directed) graph $G = (V, E)$ is defined as:

> A **walk** is an **alternating sequence of vertices and edges** of the form
> $$W = v_0, e_1, v_1, e_2, v_2, \dots, e_k, v_k$$
> where the edge $e_i$ is incident with vertices $v_{i-1}$ and $v_i$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$.

The walk **begins at vertex $v_0$** (called the *origin* or *start vertex*) and **terminates at vertex $v_k$** (called the *terminus* or *end vertex*). The **length of the walk** is defined as the **number of edges** traversed, which in this case is **$k$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** A walk is the most general form of traversal in a graph. Every trail, path, and circuit is a special case of a walk. The student is expected to know the hierarchy: **Walk ⊃ Trail ⊃ Path** (and the closed counterparts).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are taking a **road trip across Kerala on a city map** where every city is a *vertex* and every road connecting two cities is an *edge*. 

- If you travel **from Kochi → Thrissur → Palakkad → Thrissur → Kozhikode**, you have taken a **walk**. Why? Because you visited Thrissur **twice** — a walk permits revisiting vertices and edges.
- If you simply said "I drove from A to B," you are describing a walk of some finite length.
- A walk is essentially the **"any path"** category — no rules about uniqueness, no rules about repetition. It is the **"I just went somewhere by following roads"** concept.

This liberal definition is what makes walks the *starting point* for defining more restricted graph traversals like **trails** (no repeated edges) and **paths** (no repeated vertices).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Walk:** A walk in which the start vertex $v_0$ is **not equal** to the end vertex $v_k$ (i.e., $v_0 \neq v_k$).
> - The Kochi → Thrissur → Kozhikode example above is an **open walk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed Walk:** A walk in which the start vertex equals the end vertex (i.e., $v_0 = v_k$).
> - Example: Thrissur → Palakkad → Coimbatore → Thrissur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trivial Walk (Length Zero):** A walk of length $k = 0$ consisting of a single vertex $v_0$ with no edges. This is also considered a closed walk by convention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Convention):** Length of a walk is counted in terms of the **number of edges**, NOT the number of vertices. A walk with 5 vertices has length **4**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — Components of a Walk

To fully grasp the structure of a walk, decompose it as follows:

- **Vertex Sequence:** The ordered list of vertices visited: $v_0, v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k$. This sequence has $k+1$ vertices.
- **Edge Sequence:** The ordered list of edges traversed: $e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k$. This sequence has exactly $k$ edges.
- **Incident Constraint:** For every $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$, the edge $e_i$ must satisfy $e_i = \{v_{i-1}, v_i\}$ in an undirected graph, or $e_i = (v_{i-1}, v_i)$ in a directed graph.
- **Why "Alternating"?** Because a walk naturally *bounces* between a vertex and an edge — you arrive at a vertex, leave via an edge, arrive at the next vertex, and so on.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Walks

- **Why study walks?** Walks form the **theoretical foundation** for the *connectivity* of a graph. A graph is *connected* if and only if there exists a walk between **every pair of distinct vertices**.
- **How are walks counted?** The **adjacency matrix** of a graph encodes the structure, and its **$k$-th power** encodes the number of walks of length $k$ between any two vertices. This is one of the most elegant results in discrete mathematics and has direct applications in **PageRank algorithms**, **social network analysis**, and **Markov chains**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Notation** | **Condition / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Walk | $W = v_0, e_1, v_1, \dots, e_k, v_k$ | $W : v_0 \rightsquigarrow v_k$ | Edges must be incident with consecutive vertices |
| Length of walk | $k$ (number of edges) | $\vert W \vert = k$ | Always a non-negative integer |
| Number of walks of length $k$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$ | $(A^k)_{ij}$ | $A$ = adjacency matrix | $A^k$ computed via matrix multiplication |
| Open walk | $v_0 \neq v_k$ | $\vert W \vert \geq 1$ | Start and end vertices differ |
| Closed walk | $v_0 = v_k$ | $\vert W \vert \geq 1$ | Start and end vertices coincide |
| Trivial walk | $k = 0$ | $W = v_0$ | Single vertex, no edges |
| Connectivity test | $(A^k)_{ij} > 0$ for some $k$ | $\forall i, j \in V$ | There exists *some* walk iff entry is positive |
| Maximum possible walks of length $k$ | $\leq n \cdot (n-1)^k$ | $n = \vert V \vert$ | Upper bound for simple graph |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Networks (KTU CSE Focus):** Routing protocols like OSPF compute **shortest walks (paths)** between routers. The adjacency matrix power method is used in reachability analysis.
- **Web Graph & PageRank (Google Algorithm):** Treats the internet as a directed graph; a random surfer performs a walk, and the stationary distribution ranks pages.
- **Social Network Analysis (SNA):** The "six degrees of separation" concept is essentially: *in the social graph, there exists a walk of length $\leq 6$ between any two people.*
- **Compiler Design:** Control flow graphs are traversed via walks during code optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Power of a Walk" Theorem (KTU Frequently Asked):** The number of walks of length $k$ from vertex $v_i$ to vertex $v_j$ in a graph with adjacency matrix $A$ is exactly the $(i, j)$-entry of $A^k$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Walks via Adjacency Matrix Powers

This is the **flagship derivation** for the KTU board on this topic. We will prove that $(A^k)_{ij}$ counts walks of length $k$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$.

### Base Case: $k = 1$

The adjacency matrix $A = (a_{ij})_{n \times n}$ is defined as:

$$
a_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \{v_i, v_j\} \in E \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For $k = 1$, $(A^1)_{ij} = a_{ij}$. This entry is **1** if and only if edge $\{v_i, v_j\}$ exists, which corresponds to exactly one walk of length 1 from $v_i$ to $v_j$. The base case holds. ✓

### Inductive Step: From $k$ to $k+1$

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume that $(A^k)_{ij}$ counts all walks of length $k$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$ in the graph $G$.

**To Prove:** $(A^{k+1})_{ij}$ counts all walks of length $k+1$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$.

By the definition of matrix multiplication:

$$
(A^{k+1})_{ij} = (A^k \cdot A)_{ij} = \sum_{m=1}^{n} (A^k)_{im} \cdot a_{mj}
$$

**Step 1 — Interpret $(A^k)_{im}$:** By the inductive hypothesis, this is the **number of walks of length $k$** from $v_i$ to an intermediate vertex $v_m$.

**Step 2 — Interpret $a_{mj}$:** This is **1** if there is an edge from $v_m$ to $v_j$, and **0** otherwise. So $a_{mj}$ represents the *single* edge-traversal from $v_m$ to $v_j$.

**Step 3 — Interpret the product $(A^k)_{im} \cdot a_{mj}$:** This counts the number of walks of length $k$ from $v_i$ to $v_m$ **followed by** a direct edge from $v_m$ to $v_j$. This is precisely the number of walks of length $k+1$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$ **passing through $v_m$ as the penultimate vertex**.

**Step 4 — Sum over all $m$:** Summing $\sum_{m=1}^{n}$ enumerates all possible penultimate vertices $v_m$. By the **addition principle of counting**, the total is the total number of walks of length $k+1$ from $v_i$ to $v_j$.

Therefore:

$$
(A^{k+1})_{ij} = \sum_{m=1}^{n} (A^k)_{im} \cdot a_{mj} = \text{Number of walks of length } (k+1) \text{ from } v_i \text{ to } v_j
$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** Consider the graph $G$ with vertices $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and edge set $E = \{\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,3\}, \{3,4\}\}$. Find the number of walks of length 2 from vertex 1 to vertex 4.

### Step 1: Construct the Adjacency Matrix $A$

Listing edges in the order: $\{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,3\}, \{3,4\}$:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $A^2$ via Matrix Multiplication

$$
A^2 = A \cdot A
$$

**Row 1 computations:**

- $(A^2)_{11} = (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 2$
- $(A^2)_{12} = (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{13} = (0)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{14} = (0)(0) + (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$

**Row 2 computations:**

- $(A^2)_{21} = (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{22} = (1)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 2$
- $(A^2)_{23} = (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{24} = (1)(0) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$

**Row 3 computations:**

- $(A^3)_{31} = (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{32} = (1)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{33} = (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) = 3$
- $(A^2)_{34} = (1)(0) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 0$

**Row 4 computations:**

- $(A^2)_{41} = (0)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{42} = (0)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$
- $(A^2)_{43} = (0)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 0$
- $(A^2)_{44} = (0)(0) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 1$

Compiling the result:

$$
A^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Read Off the Required Entry

The number of walks of length 2 from vertex 1 to vertex 4 is:

$$
(A^2)_{14} = 1
$$

### Step 4: Verification by Enumeration

List all walks of length 2 from 1 to 4 explicitly:

- $1 \to 3 \to 4$ (via edge $\{1,3\}$ then $\{3,4\}$) ✓

This is the only such walk. The matrix method gives **1**, matching the manual count. ✓

## 3.3 Connectivity Criterion via Walks

**Theorem (Walk-based Connectivity):** A graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is **connected** if and only if every entry of the matrix

$$
M = A + A^2 + A^3 + \dots + A^{n-1}
$$

is **strictly positive** (i.e., $M_{ij} > 0$ for all $i, j$).

**Proof Sketch:** 
- If $G$ is connected, then for any pair $v_i, v_j$, there exists a path of length at most $n-1$ (a path cannot revisit vertices, so max length is $n-1$). A path is a walk, so $(A^k)_{ij} \geq 1$ for some $k \leq n-1$, making $M_{ij} \geq 1$.
- Conversely, if $M_{ij} > 0$, then there exists a walk from $v_i$ to $v_j$ of some length, which implies reachability, which implies connectivity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Walk

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef vertexNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef edgeNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef startNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    V0([V0 START VERTEX]):::startNode
    E1[EDGE E1]:::edgeNode
    V1[V1]:::vertexNode
    E2[EDGE E2]:::edgeNode
    V2[V2]:::vertexNode
    E3[EDGE E3]:::edgeNode
    V3[V3]:::vertexNode
    EK[EDGE EK]:::edgeNode
    VK([VK END VERTEX]):::endNode

    V0 --- E1
    E1 --- V1
    V1 --- E2
    E2 --- V2
    V2 --- E3
    E3 --- V3
    V3 -.-> EK
    EK --- VK

    V0 -.- noteA[/Length of walk = K edges/]
    V0 -.- noteB[/Vertices can repeat in a walk/]
    V0 -.- noteC[/Edges can repeat in a walk/]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Walk Sub-Types Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef rootNode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef level1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef level2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef level3 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    W[GRAPH WALK]:::rootNode
    OW[OPEN WALK]:::level1
    CW[CLOSED WALK]:::level1
    T[TRIVIAL WALK]:::level2
    NT[NON TRIVIAL WALK]:::level2
    TR[TRAIL]:::level3
    P[PATH]:::level3
    CIR[CIRCUIT]:::level3

    W --> OW
    W --> CW
    OW --> NT
    CW --> T
    CW --> NT
    NT --> TR
    TR --> P
    CW --> CIR

    W -.- desc1[/Most general traversal: no restrictions/]
    OW -.- desc2[/Start vertex differs from end vertex/]
    CW -.- desc3[/Start vertex equals end vertex/]
    T -.- desc4[/Length zero: single vertex, no edges/]
    TR -.- desc5[/Walk with no repeated edges/]
    P -.- desc6[/Walk with no repeated vertices/]
    CIR -.- desc7[/Closed walk with no repeated edges/]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Walk Counting via Matrix Power

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef inputBox fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    INPUT[GRAPH G WITH N VERTICES]:::inputBox
    ADJ[CONSTRUCT ADJACENCY MATRIX A]:::processBox
    POW[COMPUTE A TO POWER K]:::processBox
    READ[READ ENTRY I J FROM A K]:::processBox
    OUTPUT[NUMBER OF WALKS OF LENGTH K FROM VI TO VJ]:::outputBox

    INPUT --> ADJ
    ADJ --> POW
    POW --> READ
    READ --> OUTPUT

    INPUT -.- inNote[/Edges become 1 entries, non edges become 0/]
    POW -.- powNote[/Use standard matrix multiplication/]
    READ -.- readNote[/Position i j corresponds to vertices vi and vj/]
```
