# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GAMAT401 Module 3]**

### Part A – Question 1 [3 Marks]
**Q:** Define a *pendant vertex* and a *pendant edge* in a graph. Illustrate with an example.

**Model Answer:**

A vertex $v$ in a graph $G = (V, E)$ is called a **pendant vertex** (or *leaf*) if its degree is exactly one, i.e., $\deg(v) = 1$. The unique edge incident with $v$ is called a **pendant edge** of $G$.

**Example:** Consider the graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{a, b, c, d\}$ and edge set $E = \{(a,b), (b,c), (b,d)\}$. The degree of each vertex is $\deg(a) = 1$, $\deg(b) = 3$, $\deg(c) = 1$, $\deg(d) = 1$. Therefore, the pendant vertices are $a$, $c$, and $d$, and the pendant edges are $(a,b)$, $(b,c)$, and $(b,d)$.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition of pendant vertex, 1 mark for the definition of pendant edge, and 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### Part A – Question 2 [3 Marks]
**Q:** Is it possible for a graph to have exactly one pendant vertex? Justify.

**Model Answer:**

**No**, a graph with at least one edge cannot have exactly one pendant vertex.

**Justification:** Suppose, for contradiction, that a graph $G$ has exactly one pendant vertex $v$ of degree 1. Then the rest of the graph has $n - 1$ vertices, all of degree either 0 or $\geq 2$. The sum of degrees is:

$$\sum_{u \in V} \deg(u) = 1 + \sum_{u \neq v} \deg(u)$$

If the rest are all of even degree, the sum is odd, contradicting the Handshaking Lemma (which says the sum of degrees is $2 \lvert E \rvert$, an even number). More precisely, a graph with edges must have either **0 pendant vertices** (e.g., cycles) or **at least 2 pendant vertices** (a well-known theorem for trees and connected graphs in general).

*(Award 1 mark for the answer "No", 1 mark for the justification by Handshaking Lemma, and 1 mark for the correct conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GAMAT401 Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

### Part B – Question A [14 Marks] *(Choice 1)*

**Q:** **(a)** Define a pendant vertex and a pendant edge. Prove that a tree $T$ with $n \geq 2$ vertices has at least two pendant vertices.  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** A vertex $v$ in $T$ is a **pendant vertex** if $\deg(v) = 1$. The unique edge incident to it is a **pendant edge**.

**Proof that $T$ has at least two pendant vertices:**

Since $T$ is a connected graph with $n \geq 2$ vertices, it contains at least one path. Let $P: v_0 - v_1 - v_2 - \cdots - v_k$ be a path of **maximum length** in $T$ (so $k \geq 1$).

**Claim 1:** $v_0$ is a pendant vertex. Suppose not; then $\deg(v_0) \geq 2$, so $v_0$ has a neighbor $w$ different from $v_1$.

- If $w \notin \{v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k\}$, then $w - v_0 - v_1 - \cdots - v_k$ is a path longer than $P$, contradiction.
- If $w = v_j$ for some $j \geq 2$, then $v_0 - v_1 - \cdots - v_j - v_0$ is a cycle, contradicting that $T$ is a tree.

Hence $\deg(v_0) = 1$, so $v_0$ is pendant.

**Claim 2:** $v_k$ is also a pendant vertex, by identical reasoning applied to the other endpoint.

Therefore, $T$ has at least two distinct pendant vertices $v_0$ and $v_k$. $\blacksquare$

*(Award 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for setting up the longest-path argument, 2 marks for case analysis showing $v_0$ is pendant, 1 mark for the symmetric argument at $v_k$.)*

---

**(b)** A tree $T$ has 10 vertices, of which exactly 2 vertices have degree 3, exactly 3 vertices have degree 2, and the remaining vertices are pendant. Find: (i) the number of pendant vertices, (ii) the number of edges, and (iii) verify using the pendant count formula.  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Let $P$ be the number of pendant vertices.**

The vertices are partitioned as: $P$ pendant (degree 1), $2$ vertices of degree 3, and $3$ vertices of degree 2. Total vertices: $n = 10$.

$$P + 2 + 3 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = 5$$

So there are **5 pendant vertices**.

**Step 2 — Number of edges using the tree property.**

For a tree, $\lvert E \rvert = n - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9$ edges.

**Step 3 — Verification using the pendant count formula.**

Internal vertices are the 2 vertices of degree 3 and the 3 vertices of degree 2.

$$P(T) = 2 + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2)$$

$$P(T) = 2 + 2(3 - 2) + 3(2 - 2) = 2 + 2(1) + 3(0) = 2 + 2 + 0 = 4$$

Hmm — this gives 4, not 5. Let us re-verify the degree sequence.

**Step 4 — Re-verify using Handshaking Lemma.**

Sum of degrees:

$$\sum \deg(v) = 5(1) + 2(3) + 3(2) = 5 + 6 + 6 = 17$$

This is **odd**, which violates Handshaking Lemma. Therefore, the given degree sequence is invalid. The problem as stated has an error.

**Corrected problem:** Suppose 1 vertex has degree 3, 3 vertices have degree 2, and the rest are pendant. Then $P + 1 + 3 = 10 \Rightarrow P = 6$. Check:

$$\sum \deg(v) = 6(1) + 1(3) + 3(2) = 6 + 3 + 6 = 15 \text{ (still odd)}$$

Try: 2 vertices of degree 3, 3 vertices of degree 2, $P$ pendants, with $P = 4$:

$$4(1) + 2(3) + 3(2) = 4 + 6 + 6 = 16 \Rightarrow \lvert E \rvert = 8$$

But $n = 9$ (not 10) and $n - 1 = 8$. ✓ So with $n = 9$ vertices, $P = 4$ pendants, 2 vertices of degree 3, 3 vertices of degree 2, this is a valid tree.

**Verification by formula:**

$$P(T) = 2 + 2(3 - 2) + 3(2 - 2) = 2 + 2 + 0 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

This matches our count of 4 pendants.

*(Award 2 marks for finding $P$, 2 marks for computing edges, 1 mark for the formula application, 2 marks for verification. Note: KTU examiners will accept a valid rearrangement even if the original problem has a typo.)*

---

### Part B – Question B [14 Marks] *(Choice 2)*

**Q:** **(a)** Define pendant vertex. Prove that a connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $n - 1$ edges is a tree if and only if $G$ has no cycles.  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** A vertex $v$ in $G$ is a **pendant vertex** if $\deg(v) = 1$.

**Proof:**

**($\Rightarrow$) If $G$ is a tree, then $G$ has no cycles.**

By definition, a tree is a connected acyclic graph. Hence $G$ has no cycles. ✓

**($\Leftarrow$) If $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices, $n - 1$ edges, and no cycles, then $G$ is a tree.**

Since $G$ is already connected and has no cycles by hypothesis, we need only verify that $G$ has $n - 1$ edges (which is given) and apply the standard theorem: *a connected acyclic graph with $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges*. This is a well-known theorem proved by induction on $n$:

- **Base case** ($n = 1$): Trivially, 0 edges, 0 cycles, connected.
- **Inductive step:** Assume true for $n = k$. For a connected acyclic graph $G$ on $k + 1$ vertices, $G$ has at least one pendant vertex (proved in Part A). Removing a pendant vertex $v$ and its edge yields a connected acyclic graph on $k$ vertices, which by induction has $k - 1$ edges. Restoring $v$ and its edge gives $k$ edges in $G$. So $G$ has $k + 1 - 1 = k$ edges, completing the induction.

Since $G$ is connected, acyclic, and has $n - 1$ edges, **$G$ is a tree**. $\blacksquare$

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 3 marks for forward direction, 3 marks for reverse direction with induction.)*

---

**(b)** For a tree $T$ on $n$ vertices, derive the formula $P(T) = 2 + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2)$ where $P(T)$ is the number of pendant vertices. Hence, find the pendant count in a tree of order 8 with degree sequence $(1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 5)$.  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation:**

By the Handshaking Lemma:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \lvert E \rvert = 2(n - 1) \quad \text{(since } T \text{ is a tree)}$$

Splitting the sum:

$$\sum_{\text{pendant } v} \deg(v) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} \deg(v) = 2(n - 1)$$

The pendant contribution is $P(T) \cdot 1 = P(T)$. So:

$$P(T) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} \deg(v) = 2(n - 1)$$

Rewriting the internal sum as $2 + (\deg(v) - 2)$ for each internal vertex:

$$P(T) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} \big[2 + (\deg(v) - 2)\big] = 2(n - 1)$$

$$P(T) + 2(n - P(T)) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2) = 2(n - 1)$$

$$P(T) + 2n - 2P(T) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2) = 2n - 2$$

$$-P(T) + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2) = -2$$

$$\boxed{P(T) = 2 + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2)}$$

**Application to the given tree:**

$n = 8$, degree sequence $(1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 5)$.

- Pendant vertices (degree 1): **4** (the first four entries).
- Internal vertices and their degrees: $2, 2, 3, 5$.

**Verification of tree property first:**

Sum of degrees: $4 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 5 = 16$. Edges: $\lvert E \rvert = 8$. Required for tree: $n - 1 = 7$. So $\lvert E \rvert = 8 \neq 7$, which means **this is not a tree** (it has exactly one cycle).

**Hence the formula for trees does not directly apply.** However, we can still count pendants by inspection: **$P = 4$**.

If the intended degree sequence was $(1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 4)$, sum $= 4 + 6 + 4 = 14$, $\lvert E \rvert = 7 = n - 1$ ✓. Then internal vertices are $2, 2, 2, 4$:

$$P(T) = 2 + (2 - 2) + (2 - 2) + (2 - 2) + (4 - 2) = 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

*(Award 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for identification of pendants, 2 marks for verification using the formula.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing pendant with isolated vertices:** A vertex of degree 0 is *isolated*, not pendant. Always check $\deg(v) = 1$, not just "low degree."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Handshaking parity check:** Before applying the pendant formula, always verify that the sum of degrees is even. If it is odd, the degree sequence is impossible.
>
> 3. **Misapplying the formula to non-trees:** The formula $P(T) = 2 + \sum (\deg(v) - 2)$ holds **only for trees**. For general graphs, you must enumerate pendants directly by checking each degree.
>
> 4. **Off-by-one errors in edge counts:** A tree of order $n$ has exactly $n - 1$ edges. Writing $n$ or $n - 2$ loses a mark immediately.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to state $n \geq 2$:** The theorem "every tree has at least two pendants" requires $n \geq 2$. For $n = 1$ (a single vertex), there are 0 pendants. Always state the hypothesis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pendant Vertices**

- **Definition:** A vertex $v$ in $G$ is a *pendant vertex* (or *leaf*) if $\deg(v) = 1$.
- **Pendant edge:** The unique edge incident with a pendant vertex.
- **Support vertex:** The neighbor of a pendant vertex (degree $\geq 2$ in trees).
- **Isolated vs. Pendant:** Isolated vertices have $\deg(v) = 0$; pendant vertices have $\deg(v) = 1$. They are **distinct concepts**.
- **Key Theorem:** Every tree with $n \geq 2$ vertices has at least **2 pendant vertices**.
- **Proof technique:** Use the longest path in the tree; both endpoints must be pendant.
- **Pendant Count Formula (trees only):** $P(T) = 2 + \sum_{\text{internal } v} (\deg(v) - 2)$.
- **Bounds on pendants in a tree:** $2 \leq P(T) \leq n - 1$.
- **Maximum pendants:** Star graph $K_{1, n-1}$ with $n - 1$ pendants.
- **Minimum pendants:** Path graph $P_n$ with exactly 2 pendants.
- **Cycle graph $C_n$:** Has **0 pendant vertices** (every vertex has degree 2).
- **Complete graph $K_n$:** Has **0 pendant vertices** for $n \geq 2$.
- **Handshaking Lemma:** $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \lvert E \rvert$ — must always be **even**.
- **Tree edge count:** A tree of order $n$ has exactly $n - 1$ edges.
- **Removing a pendant vertex from a tree:** Yields a smaller tree (or isolated vertex).
- **Algorithm use:** Pendant vertices are starting points for *tree pruning*, *leaf-to-root traversal*, and *topological sorting* in DAGs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance and Centres in a Tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science 4 > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Distance and Centres in a Tree # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Preliminary: What is a Tree? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Tree):** A **tree** $T = (V, E)$ is a connected, acyclic (cycle-free) undirected graph. Equivalently, a tree is a connected graph in which there exists **exactly one** path between every pair of vertices. **Essential Properties of a Tree with $n$ vertices:** - $T$ has exactly $n - 1$ edges. - $T$ is connected and contains no cycles. - Adding any edge to $T$ creates exactly one cycle. - Removing any edge from $T$ disconnects it. - Every tree with at least two vertices has at least two **leaves** (vertices of degree 1). > [!NOTE] > **Degree of a vertex** $v$, denoted $\deg(v)$, is the number of edges incident to $v$. A **leaf** is a vertex of degree 1. A **pendant edge** is the unique edge incident to a leaf. ## 1.2 Distance in a Tree > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Distance):** The **distance** $d(u, v)$ between two vertices $u$ and $v$ in a tree is the **length (number of edges)** of the unique path connecting $u$ and $v$. **Distance satisfies the metric axioms for any three vertices $u, v, w$:** 1. **Non-negativity:** $d(u, v) \geq 0$, with equality iff $u = v$. 2. **Symmetry:** $d(u, v) = d(v, u)$. 3. **Triangle Inequality:** $d(u, w) \leq d(u, v) + d(v, w)$. ## 1.3 Eccentricity, Radius, Diameter and Centre > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Eccentricity):** The **eccentricity** of a vertex $v$ in a tree $T$ is the maximum distance from $v$ to any other vertex in $T$: > $$e(v) \;=\; \max_{u \in V(T)} \, d(v, u)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Radius):** The **radius** of a tree $T$ is the **minimum** eccentricity among all its vertices: > $$r(T) \;=\; \min_{v \in V(T)} \, e(v)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Diameter):** The **diameter** of a tree $T$ is the **maximum** eccentricity among all its vertices, equivalently, the length of the longest path in $T$: > $$d(T) \;=\; \max_{v \in V(T)} \, e(v) \;=\; \max_{u, v \in V(T)} \, d(u, v)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Centre):** A vertex $c \in V(T)$ is a **centre** of $T$ if its eccentricity equals the radius: $e(c) = r(T)$. The set of all centres is denoted: > $$C(T) \;=\; \{\, v \in V(T) \mid e(v) = r(T) \,\}$$ --- ## 🌳 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hospital" Intuition Imagine a network of villages connected by single-lane roads (no loops) — that is your **tree**. Now build a **hospital** in some village $v$. The **eccentricity** $e(v)$ is the **worst-case travel time** for any villager to reach the hospital (the farthest village determines the worst case). The **radius** $r(T)$ is the smallest possible such worst-case time, achieved by **placing the hospital at the best location** — that best location is the **centre**. The **diameter** $d(T)$ is the worst-case travel time when the hospital is placed at the *worst* possible village. **Key Insight:** The centre is the *best* place to put a service facility (hospital, server, broadcasting tower), while the diameter tells you the *longest* response time in the worst case. Hence, **minimising the diameter** is a core objective in **network design**. ## 1.4 Notation Summary | Symbol | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $V(T)$ | Set of vertices of the tree $T$ | | $E(T)$ | Set of edges of the tree $T$ | | $n = \vert V(T) \vert$ | Number of vertices | | $d(u, v)$ | Distance between $u$ and $v$ (number of edges) | | $e(v)$ | Eccentricity of vertex $v$ | | $r(T)$ | Radius of $T$ | | $d(T)$ | Diameter of $T$ | | $C(T)$ | Set of centres of $T$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Relationship between Radius and Diameter > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem 1 (Radius–Diameter Inequality):** For every tree $T$, > $$r(T) \;\leq\; d(T) \;\leq\; 2 \, r(T)$$ **Reasoning behind the inequality:** - **Left inequality:** $r(T) = \min_v e(v) \leq e(v)$ for every $v$; hence $r(T) \leq \max_v e(v) = d(T)$. - **Right inequality:** Let $u, v$ be endpoints of a longest path (so $d(u, v) = d(T)$). Take any vertex $w$ on this path. By the triangle inequality, $$d(u, v) \;\leq\; d(u, w) + d(w, v) \;\leq\; e(w) + e(w) \;=\; 2 e(w).$$ So $d(T) \leq 2 e(w)$ for every $w$. In particular, $d(T) \leq 2 e(c) = 2 r(T)$ where $c$ is a centre. ## 2.2 The Centre Theorem (Cardinality of $C(T)$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem 2 (Jordan's Centre Theorem for Trees):** The centre of a tree consists of **either one vertex or two adjacent vertices** (i.e., $\vert C(T) \vert \in \{1, 2\}$). **Intuitive Reasoning (Why?):** The centre must lie in the *middle* of every longest path. If the diameter path has odd length, the middle is a single vertex; if even, the middle is the unique edge between two central vertices. No other vertex can have minimum eccentricity. ## 2.3 Properties of Endpoints of a Longest Path > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem 3 (Endpoints of Longest Path):** Every endpoint of a longest path in a tree $T$ is a vertex of **maximum eccentricity** equal to $d(T)$. These endpoints are always **leaves** of the tree. **Why?** Any leaf $u$ that is an endpoint of a longest path has $e(u) = d(T)$ because the farthest vertex from $u$ lies at the other end of that path. ## 2.4 Jordan's Algorithm: Finding the Centre of a Tree > [!IMPORTANT] > **Algorithm (Repeated Leaf-Stripping):** The centre of a tree can be found by: > 1. Identify all leaves of $T$ (vertices of degree 1). > 2. Remove all leaves and their incident edges **simultaneously**. > 3. The remaining graph is again a tree (or empty/isolated vertices). > 4. Repeat steps 1–3 until **one vertex or two adjacent vertices** remain. > 5. The remaining vertex/vertices form $C(T)$. **Why this works:** Removing all leaves shortens every path by exactly 1 from both ends. Hence the diameter shrinks by 2, and the centre remains invariant throughout the process. This is a constructive proof of Theorem 2. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Number of edges in a tree | $\vert E(T) \vert = n - 1$ | $n$ = number of vertices | | Distance | $d(u, v)$ = number of edges on the unique $u$–$v$ path | Always $\geq 0$ | | Eccentricity | $e(v) = \max_{u} d(v, u)$ | Max distance from $v$ | | Radius | $r(T) = \min_{v} e(v)$ | Min eccentricity | | Diameter | $d(T) = \max_{v} e(v)$ | Max eccentricity / longest path length | | Centre | $C(T) = \{ v \in V \mid e(v) = r(T) \}$ | $\vert C(T) \vert \in \{1, 2\}$ | | Radius–Diameter Bound | $r(T) \leq d(T) \leq 2 r(T)$ | Always holds | | Leaf endpoint property | $e(\text{leaf endpoint}) = d(T)$ | Endpoints of longest path are leaves | | Center parity rule | Odd diameter $\Rightarrow$ one centre; Even diameter $\Rightarrow$ two centres | Distance is integer, so parity decides | ## 2.6 Engineering and Real-World Applications - **Computer Networks:** The *centre* of a network topology (a tree-shaped LAN/MAN) is the optimal location to place a **server, router, or data center** to minimise the maximum response time. - **Telecommunications:** Diameter of a tree-network gives the **worst-case latency**. Tree topologies (spanning trees in MST algorithms) often approximate optimal broadcasting. - **Distributed Systems:** The **centroid** (different from centre, minimises average distance) and **centre** of a spanning tree guide the placement of **leader nodes** in hierarchical consensus protocols. - **VLSI Design:** Tree-based clock distribution networks use the centre of the tree as the **clock source**, minimising the maximum skew to all flip-flops. - **Phylogenetics:** Evolutionary trees use diameter-based metrics to estimate **divergence times** between species. - **File Systems / Hierarchical Databases:** Centred trees yield **balanced** access costs in B-tree-like structures. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing Eccentricity, Radius, Diameter and Centre Consider the following tree $T$ with 8 vertices: $$T : \quad A \text{ (root)} \longrightarrow \{B, C\}, \quad B \longrightarrow \{D, E\}, \quad C \longrightarrow \{F\}, \quad D \longrightarrow \{G\}, \quad E \longrightarrow \{H\}.$$ Equivalently, edge set $E(T) = \{AB, AC, BD, BE, CF, DG, EH\}$ and $V(T) = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\}$. ### Step 1 — Build the full distance matrix For each ordered pair, count the edges on the unique path: | $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **A** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | | **B** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | | **C** | 1 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 4 | | **D** | 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | | **E** | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 1 | | **F** | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 5 | 5 | | **G** | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | 5 | 0 | 4 | | **H** | 3 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 0 | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** Each entry counts the number of edges traversed in the unique path between the two vertices. For example, $d(A, G)$: $A \to B \to D \to G$ is 3 edges, so $d(A, G) = 3$. ### Step 2 — Extract eccentricities (row maximums) | Vertex $v$ | $e(v) = \max_u d(v, u)$ | | :---: | :---: | | A | $\max\{0,1,1,2,2,2,3,3\} = 3$ | | B | $\max\{1,0,2,1,1,3,2,2\} = 3$ | | C | $\max\{1,2,0,3,3,1,4,4\} = 4$ | | D | $\max\{2,1,3,0,2,4,1,3\} = 4$ | | E | $\max\{2,1,3,2,0,4,3,1\} = 4$ | | F | $\max\{2,3,1,4,4,0,5,5\} = 5$ | | G | $\max\{3,2,4,1,3,5,0,4\} = 5$ | | H | $\max\{3,2,4,3,1,5,4,0\} = 5$ | ### Step 3 — Compute radius, diameter, and identify the centre $$\begin{aligned} r(T) &= \min_{v} e(v) = \min\{3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5\} = 3, \\ d(T) &= \max_{v} e(v) = \max\{3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5\} = 5, \\ C(T) &= \{\, v \in V \mid e(v) = 3 \,\} = \{A, B\}. \end{aligned}$$ **Verification of the bound:** $3 \leq 5 \leq 2 \cdot 3 = 6$ ✓ (satisfies $r(T) \leq d(T) \leq 2 r(T)$). **Longest path (diameter path):** $G \to D \to B \to A \to C \to F$ (length 5). The middle edge of this path is the edge $A$–$B$, so the centres are $A$ and $B$ — adjacent vertices, matching Theorem 2. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Centre Types by Diameter Parity | Diameter $d(T)$ | Parity | Number of Centres $\vert C(T) \vert$ | Position of Centres | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | odd | 1 | The single edge's two endpoints (the tree is $K_2$ when $n=2$); for path $P_2$ centre is the middle edge's vertices — but typically $n \geq 3$ | | 2 | even | 1 or 2 | A "star" with central vertex as sole centre (e.g., $K_{1,k}$) | | 3 | odd | 1 | A single middle vertex of the diameter path | | 4 | even | 1 or 2 | One or two middle vertices of the diameter path | | $2k$ (even) | even | 1 or 2 | At the central position(s) of the longest path | | $2k+1$ (odd) | odd | 2 | The two middle adjacent vertices of the longest path | ## 3.3 Detailed Proof: The Centre Theorem (Theorem 2) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Claim:** For any tree $T$, the centre $C(T)$ has exactly one or two vertices, and if two, they are adjacent. **Proof by Construction (Jordan's Algorithm):** Let $T_0 = T$. Define inductively: $T_{i+1}$ is the tree obtained from $T_i$ by deleting **all leaves** of $T_i$ and their incident edges. **Step 1 — $T_{i+1}$ is still a tree (or empty).** Removing leaves and their incident edges from a tree cannot create a cycle and cannot disconnect a tree (since the leaf was a pendant vertex connected by exactly one edge). Hence the result is again a tree (assuming $T_i$ had $\geq 2$ vertices). **Step 2 — Effect on distances and eccentricities.** For any two vertices $u, v$ in $T_{i+1}$ (i.e., non-leaves of $T_i$), $$d_{T_{i+1}}(u, v) \;=\; d_{T_i}(u, v) - 2,$$ because every longest path between $u$ and $v$ in $T_i$ is extended at both ends by the leaf-chains that were stripped. Therefore the diameter and radius shrink by 2 each round: $$d(T_{i+1}) = d(T_i) - 2, \qquad r(T_{i+1}) = r(T_i) - 2.$$ **Step 3 — Centre invariance.** A vertex $v$ in $T_{i+1}$ is a centre of $T_{i+1}$ iff it was a centre of $T_i$, because eccentricity is reduced by the same constant 2 for all surviving vertices: $$e_{T_{i+1}}(v) = e_{T_i}(v) - 2.$$ **Step 4 — Termination.** A tree with $n$ vertices becomes empty after at most $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ rounds. Eventually, we reach a tree $T_k$ with either: - **One vertex** (when the original tree had an odd-length diameter), or - **Two vertices connected by one edge** (when the original tree had an even-length diameter). These surviving vertex/vertices are precisely the centres of $T$. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "two adjacent vertices"?** If the final tree $T_k$ is a single edge $\{x, y\}$, both $x$ and $y$ are centres of $T_k$ (each has eccentricity 1, which is the radius of a 2-vertex tree). They are obviously adjacent. If $T_k$ is a single vertex $v$, then $v$ is the unique centre. ## 3.4 Worked Example: Applying Jordan's Algorithm Reusing the tree from §3.1. Vertices: $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\}$ with edges $\{AB, AC, BD, BE, CF, DG, EH\}$. **Round 0 (Initial):** $T_0 = T$. - Leaves of $T_0$ (degree 1): $G, H, F$. - $T_0$: $$A \text{—} B \text{—} D \text{—} G, \quad A \text{—} B \text{—} E \text{—} H, \quad A \text{—} C \text{—} F.$$ **Round 1:** Remove $\{G, H, F\}$ and edges $DG, EH, CF$. - Remaining vertices: $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$. - Leaves of $T_1$ (now): $C, D, E$ (each has degree 1 in $T_1$). **Round 2:** Remove $\{C, D, E\}$ and edges $AC, BD, BE$. - Remaining vertices: $\{A, B\}$. - Edge: $AB$. **Termination:** $T_2 = \{A, B\}$ with one edge. This is a 2-vertex tree. **Result:** $C(T) = \{A, B\}$ ✓ (matches the eccentricity method in §3.1). > [!TIP] > **Counting the rounds:** Since the diameter was 5 (odd), the algorithm terminated with two adjacent vertices. The number of rounds is $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$. ## 3.5 Auxiliary Lemma: Longest Path Always has Leaves as Endpoints > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lemma:** If $P$ is a longest path in a tree $T$ with endpoints $u$ and $v$, then $\deg(u) = \deg(v) = 1$ (both endpoints are leaves of $T$). **Proof sketch:** Suppose $\deg(u) \geq 2$. Then $u$ has a neighbour $w$ not on $P$ (otherwise $u$ would lie on a cycle). Appending the edge $uw$ extends $P$, contradicting maximality. Hence $u$ must be a leaf. Symmetric argument for $v$. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Centre-Finding Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Input Tree T] --> stepB[Build Distance Matrix d u,v] stepB --> stepC[Compute Eccentricity e v for all v] stepC --> stepD[Find r T and d T from e values] stepD --> stepE[Identify Centres C T equals v with e v equals r T] stepE --> stepF[Verify by Jordan's Leaf Stripping] stepF --> stepG[Output: r T, d T, C T] stepAlt[Alternative: Apply Jordan's Algorithm] --> stepH[Round 0: Identify all leaves L0] stepH --> stepI[Remove L0 and incident edges] stepI --> stepJ{1 or 2 vertices remain?} stepJ -- No --> stepK[Identify new leaves L1 and remove] stepK --> stepJ stepJ -- Yes --> stepL[Remaining vertex/vertices = C T] stepL --> stepG ``` ## 4.2 Diagram: The Worked-Example Tree with Eccentricities ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[A
e=3 CENTRE] nodeB[B
e=3 CENTRE] nodeC[C
e=4] nodeD[D
e=4] nodeE[E

e=5] nodeG[G
e=5] nodeH[H
e=5] nodeA --- nodeB nodeA --- nodeC nodeB --- nodeD nodeB --- nodeE nodeC --- nodeF nodeD --- nodeG nodeE --- nodeH ``` **Reading the diagram:** Vertices $A$ and $B$ are highlighted as **CENTRE** with the smallest eccentricity $3$. The leaves $F, G, H$ carry the largest eccentricity $5 = d(T)$. The diameter path is $G \to D \to B \to A \to C \to F$ (length 5). ## 4.3 Diagram: Iterative Leaf-Stripping on the Worked-Example Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph round0[Round 0 - Original Tree T0] r0A[A] --- r0B[B] r0A --- r0C[C] r0B --- r0D[D] r0B --- r0E[E] r0C --- r0F[F] r0D --- r0G[G] r0E --- r0H[H] end subgraph round1[Round 1 - Remove leaves G H F] r1A[A] --- r1B[B] r1A --- r1C[C] r1B --- r1D[D] r1B --- r1E[E] end subgraph round2[Round 2 - Remove leaves C D E] r2A[A] --- r2B[B] end round0 --> round1 --> round2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Each round, the leaves (redundant pendant vertices) are stripped. The surviving graph remains a tree. After 2 rounds, the tree reduces to a single edge $\{A, B\}$, identifying the two adjacent centres. ## 4.4 Diagram: Radius-Diameter Parity Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD startD[Compute Diameter d T] --> parQ{d T is odd or even?} parQ -- Odd --> oddCase[Two adjacent centres
located at middle edge of longest path] parQ -- Even --> evenCase{Number of middle vertices} evenCase -- 1 vertex --> oneCentre[Unique centre
at the middle vertex of longest path] evenCase -- 2 vertices --> twoCentres[Two adjacent centres
at the two middle vertices of longest path] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Define** the terms *eccentricity*, *radius*, and *diameter* of a tree. State the relationship that always holds between the radius and the diameter. **Model Answer:** - **Eccentricity of a vertex $v$:** $e(v) = \max_{u \in V(T)} d(v, u)$ — the maximum distance from $v$ to any other vertex. - **Radius of $T$:** $r(T) = \min_{v \in V(T)} e(v)$ — the smallest eccentricity. - **Diameter of $T$:** $d(T) = \max_{v \in V(T)} e(v)$ — the largest eccentricity, equal to the length of the longest path. **Relationship (Radius–Diameter Inequality):** For any tree $T$, $$r(T) \;\leq\; d(T) \;\leq\; 2 \, r(T).$$ This follows because the minimum eccentricity is at most every eccentricity, and the longest path is at most twice the minimum eccentricity (by the triangle inequality applied to a centre vertex). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **State** Jordan's theorem on the centre of a tree. What is the maximum possible number of centres in a tree? **Model Answer:** > **Jordan's Centre Theorem:** The centre of a tree consists of either **one vertex** or **two adjacent vertices**. Hence, the maximum possible number of centres in a tree is $\boxed{2}$. The centres are always found at the middle of every longest path: if the diameter has odd length, the middle is a single vertex; if even, the middle is an edge whose endpoints are the two centres. *(Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > Consider the tree $T$ with vertex set $V(T) = \{P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, W\}$ and edge set $E(T) = \{PQ, PR, QS, QT, RU, SV, TW\}$. > > **(a)** Compute the eccentricity of every vertex, the radius $r(T)$, and the diameter $d(T)$. Hence identify the centre(s) $C(T)$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Apply Jordan's leaf-stripping algorithm to $T$ and verify that the result matches the centre(s) found in part (a). Justify why the algorithm terminates with one or two vertices. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Identify the tree structure.** - Edges: $PQ, PR, QS, QT, RU, SV, TW$. - Adjacency: $P \to \{Q, R\}$, $Q \to \{P, S, T\}$, $R \to \{P, U\}$, $S \to \{Q, V\}$, $T \to \{Q, W\}$, $U \to \{R\}$, $V \to \{S\}$, $W \to \{T\}$. **Step 2 — Construct the distance matrix.** For each pair, count edges on the unique path. | $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ | P | Q | R | S | T | U | V | W | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **P** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | | **Q** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | | **R** | 1 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 4 | | **S** | 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | | **T** | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 1 | | **U** | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 5 | 5 | | **V** | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | 5 | 0 | 4 | | **W** | 3 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 0 | **Step 3 — Extract eccentricities** (row-wise maximums): | Vertex | $e(v)$ | | :---: | :---: | | P | 3 | | Q | 3 | | R | 4 | | S | 4 | | T | 4 | | U | 5 | | V | 5 | | W | 5 | **Step 4 — Compute radius, diameter, and identify centres.** $$\begin{aligned} r(T) &= \min\{3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5\} = 3, \\ d(T) &= \max\{3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5\} = 5, \\ C(T) &= \{v \in V \mid e(v) = 3\} = \{P, Q\}. \end{aligned}$$ **Verification of the bound:** $3 \leq 5 \leq 6$ ✓. Centres $P$ and $Q$ are adjacent. ✓ *(Bloom Level: Apply)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Round 0.** Leaves of $T$ are vertices of degree 1: $U, V, W$. Remove $\{U, V, W\}$ and edges $RU, SV, TW$. - Remaining tree $T_1$: vertices $\{P, Q, R, S, T\}$ with edges $\{PQ, PR, QS, QT\}$. **Step 2 — Round 1.** In $T_1$, leaves are vertices of degree 1: $R, S, T$. Remove $\{R, S, T\}$ and edges $PR, QS, QT$. - Remaining tree $T_2$: vertices $\{P, Q\}$ with single edge $PQ$. **Step 3 — Termination.** $T_2$ has 2 vertices, so the algorithm terminates. $C(T) = \{P, Q\}$. ✓ Matches Part (a). **Justification of termination:** Each round strictly reduces the number of vertices. After at most $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ rounds, the tree has $\leq 2$ vertices. Because every longest path is shortened by 2 edges per round, the diameter reduces as $d(T_{i+1}) = d(T_i) - 2$. Once $d(T_k) \in \{0, 1\}$, the tree is a single vertex or a single edge, and these survivors are the centres. The algorithm always produces **1 centre** (if final diameter is 0, i.e., one vertex left) or **2 adjacent centres** (if final diameter is 1, i.e., one edge left). *(Bloom Level: Analyze)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a)** Prove that the centre of a tree $T$ consists of either one vertex or two adjacent vertices. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For the tree $T$ with $V(T) = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\}$ and $E(T) = \{AB, AC, BD, BE, CF, DG, EH\}$, find the radius, diameter, and the centre(s) using Jordan's algorithm. Justify each round. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — Proof of Jordan's Centre Theorem **Statement:** For any tree $T$, the centre $C(T)$ has exactly one or two vertices, and if two, they are adjacent. **Proof using Jordan's Algorithm:** Define $T_0 = T$, and inductively let $T_{i+1}$ be obtained from $T_i$ by deleting all leaves of $T_i$ and their incident edges. **(i) $T_{i+1}$ is a tree (or empty).** Since $T_i$ is a tree, removing leaves (vertices of degree 1) and pendant edges cannot create cycles and cannot disconnect the remaining vertices (a leaf contributes only one connection). Thus the resulting graph is either a tree or empty. **(ii) Diameter reduction by 2.** Let $u, v$ be the endpoints of a longest path in $T_i$. Then $\deg_{T_i}(u) = \deg_{T_i}(v) = 1$ (by the Lemma in §3.5). So both $u$ and $v$ are leaves in $T_i$ and are removed in round $i+1$. The longest path in $T_{i+1}$ is obtained by trimming one edge from each end of the longest path in $T_i$. Therefore: $$d(T_{i+1}) = d(T_i) - 2.$$ **(iii) Eccentricity reduction by 2 for surviving vertices.** For any vertex $x \in V(T_{i+1})$, the longest path from $x$ in $T_i$ passes through some leaf, so it loses 1 edge in the new graph. The shortest path to any other surviving vertex loses 1 edge from each end. Therefore: $$e_{T_{i+1}}(x) = e_{T_i}(x) - 2 \quad \text{for all } x \in V(T_{i+1}).$$ Consequently, **the relative ordering of eccentricities is preserved**, and the set of centres is invariant: $C(T_{i+1}) = C(T_i)$. **(iv) Termination.** A tree with $n$ vertices has at most $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ leaves. Each round removes at least one vertex, so the process terminates in $\leq n$ rounds. Eventually, $T_k$ has $1$ or $2$ vertices. If $1$ vertex remains, it is the **unique centre**. If $2$ vertices remain, both are centres of $T_k$ (eccentricity $1$ each) and they are **adjacent** (connected by the only remaining edge). Thus $C(T) = C(T_0) = C(T_k)$ has either 1 or 2 adjacent vertices. $\blacksquare$ *(Bloom Level: Analyze / Evaluate)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Jordan's Algorithm The tree is identical to the worked example in §3.1 and §3.4. **Round 0:** $T_0 = T$. Leaves (degree 1): $G, H, F$. Remove them with edges $DG, EH, CF$. - $T_1$: vertices $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$, edges $\{AB, AC, BD, BE\}$. **Round 1:** Leaves of $T_1$ (degree 1): $C, D, E$. Remove them with edges $AC, BD, BE$. - $T_2$: vertices $\{A, B\}$, edge $AB$. **Termination:** $T_2$ has 2 vertices with 1 edge. Both are centres. **Results:** $$r(T) = 3, \quad d(T) = 5, \quad C(T) = \{A, B\}.$$ **Justification per round:** - *Why are $G, H, F$ leaves in $T_0$?* Each has degree 1: $G$ connects only to $D$, $H$ only to $E$, $F$ only to $C$. - *Why are $C, D, E$ leaves in $T_1$?* After removing $G, H, F$, vertices $C, D, E$ each have only one surviving neighbour ($C \to A$, $D \to B$, $E \to B$). - *Why does the algorithm terminate at $\{A, B\}$?* $A$ and $B$ are mutually adjacent with no other surviving vertices; this is a 2-vertex tree $K_2$. The two centres are adjacent — consistent with Jordan's theorem. *(Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting symmetry of distance:** Many students compute $d(u, v)$ correctly but skip the reverse $d(v, u)$, leading to errors in the eccentricity matrix. The distance is symmetric; always fill both halves. > 2. **Confusing "centre" with "centroid":** KTU examiners deduct marks if you mix up the *centre* (minimises maximum distance) with the *centroid* (minimises sum of distances). Centre uses eccentricity; centroid uses total distance. > 3. **Skipping verification:** Always verify that the centres found by the leaf-stripping algorithm match the centres found via eccentricities. Marks are reserved for the *cross-check*. > 4. **Ignoring the radius–diameter bound:** State and verify $r(T) \leq d(T) \leq 2r(T)$. Examiners expect this as a sanity check. > 5. **Claiming "more than 2 centres" is possible:** This is a frequent error. Always invoke Jordan's theorem to rule it out. > 6. **Not labelling leaves explicitly:** When using Jordan's algorithm, list the leaves at each round and justify why they have degree 1 in the current tree. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🌲 **Tree** = connected + acyclic; has $n-1$ edges; unique path between any two vertices. - 📏 **Distance** $d(u, v)$ = number of edges on the unique $u$–$v$ path; satisfies metric axioms. - 🎯 **Eccentricity** $e(v) = \max_u d(v, u)$. - ⭕ **Radius** $r(T) = \min_v e(v)$; **Diameter** $d(T) = \max_v e(v)$ = length of longest path. - ⭐ **Centre** $C(T) = \{v \mid e(v) = r(T)\}$. - ⚖️ **Radius–Diameter Inequality:** $r(T) \leq d(T) \leq 2r(T)$ (always). - 🏆 **Jordan's Theorem:** $\vert C(T) \vert \in \{1, 2\}$; if 2, they are adjacent. - 🔄 **Jordan's Algorithm:** Repeatedly remove all leaves. Surviving 1 or 2 vertices = the centre(s). - 🍃 **Endpoint Lemma:** Both endpoints of a longest path are leaves with $e(v) = d(T)$. - 🎨 **Parity rule:** Odd diameter ⇒ 1 or 2 adjacent centres at middle edge; even diameter ⇒ 1 centre at middle vertex (or 2 middle vertices in special cases). - 🛠️ **Applications:** Network design, server placement, VLSI clock distribution, phylogenetic trees, hierarchical databases. - ✅ **Cross-check:** Always verify that leaf-stripping result matches eccentricity-based computation. - 🚫 **Common confusion:** Centre $\neq$ Centroid. Centre minimises *maximum* distance; centroid minimises *sum* of distances.

topics.title: Rooted and Binary Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science  4
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rooted and Binary Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rooted Tree — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rosen, Discrete Mathematics & Its Applications):**
> A **rooted tree** is a tree in which one vertex has been designated as the **root** and every edge is directed *away from* the root. The root is the only vertex with no parent, while every other vertex has exactly one parent.

Every rooted tree $T$ with vertex set $V(T)$ and edge set $E(T)$ satisfies the following foundational constraints:

$$
|V(T)| - 1 \;=\; |E(T)|
$$

The root $r \in V(T)$ has **in-degree = 0** and **out-degree $\geq 0$**. All other vertices have **in-degree = 1**.

### Hierarchical Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Description |
|---|---|
| **Parent** | A vertex $u$ is the parent of $v$ if there is a directed edge from $u$ to $v$. |
| **Child** | If $u$ is the parent of $v$, then $v$ is a child of $u$. |
| **Siblings** | Vertices that share the same parent. |
| **Ancestors** | All vertices on the unique path from the root to $v$. |
| **Descendants** | All vertices $w$ for which $v$ lies on the path from root to $w$. |
| **Leaf** | A vertex with **no children** (out-degree $= 0$). |
| **Internal Vertex** | A vertex with at least one child. |
| **Subtree** | A tree consisting of a vertex and all its descendants. |
| **Height** $h(T)$ | Length (in edges) of the longest path from root to a leaf. |
| **Level** $\ell(v)$ | Length of the unique path from the root to $v$. |

> [!NOTE]
> The root is conventionally placed at **Level 0** in KTU board problems.

## 1.2 Binary Tree — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **binary tree** $T$ is a rooted tree in which every vertex has **at most two children**, designated as the **left child** and the **right child**. The two children are distinguishable; the subtree rooted at the left child is the **left subtree**, and the subtree rooted at the right child is the **right subtree**.

A binary tree is **proper** (or **full**) if every internal vertex has exactly two children. A binary tree is **strictly binary** when it is a proper binary tree with no vertices of degree 1 except possibly the root (this is a stricter version used in KTU proof questions).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition First

Think of a **rooted tree** as a **family pedigree chart** that flows downward in one direction only:

- The **root** = the oldest ancestor (great-grandfather) at the top.
- **Edges** = direct parent–child relationships, drawn as arrows going *down* generation by generation.
- **Leaves** = the youngest generation, with no offspring below them.
- **Binary tree** = a special pedigree where each ancestor has **at most 2 children** (a left heir and a right heir), like the line of succession in a monarchy.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world instantiations of binary trees in Computer Science:**
> - **Binary Search Trees (BSTs)** — used in databases for $O(\log n)$ lookup.
> - **Expression trees** — compilers parse arithmetic expressions using binary trees.
> - **Huffman coding trees** — used in data compression (ZIP, JPEG).
> - **Decision trees** — used in machine learning classifiers.
> - **File systems** — directory hierarchies (B-trees, Red-Black trees).

## 1.4 Types of Binary Trees (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Type | Definition | Mark Weightage (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Binary Tree** | Every internal node has exactly 2 children. | High — appears in Part B. |
| **Complete Binary Tree** | All levels completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled **left to right**. | High — frequently tested. |
| **Perfect Binary Tree** | All internal nodes have 2 children **and** all leaves are at the same level. | Medium. |
| **Skewed Binary Tree** | Every node has only one child — either all-left or all-right. | Medium. |
| **Balanced Binary Tree** | Height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is $\leq 1$. | Medium-High. |

## 1.5 Visual Sketch of a Sample Binary Tree

```
              [A]  ← root, Level 0
             /   \
           [B]   [C]   Level 1
          /   \     \
        [D]   [E]   [F]  Level 2
              /     / \
            [G]   [H] [I]  Level 3
```
In this tree: $A$ is the root, $B$ and $C$ are siblings, $D,E,F,G,H,I$ are leaves. The height is $h(T) = 3$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Counting Theorem for Rooted Trees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 1 (Sum of Degrees = 2|E|):**
> If $T$ is a rooted tree with $n$ vertices and $e$ edges, then the sum of out-degrees of all vertices equals $e$, and the sum of all degrees equals $2e$.

**Proof Logic (Why?):** In any tree, every vertex except the root has exactly one incoming edge. Therefore the number of edges equals $n - 1$. Each edge contributes exactly **1 to the out-degree of some vertex** and **1 to the in-degree of another vertex**, hence the total degree sum is $2e = 2(n-1)$.

## 2.2 Key Theorems for Binary Trees (KTU Most-Frequently Asked)

### Theorem 2 — Edges in any Tree
If $T$ is a tree with $n$ vertices, then the number of edges is **exactly**:
$$e = n - 1$$

### Theorem 3 — Leaves and Internal Nodes
If $T$ is a **full binary tree** with $i$ internal vertices, then the number of leaves is:
$$L = i + 1$$
Equivalently, if the tree has $n$ total vertices with $L$ leaves and $i$ internal nodes:
$$n = 2i + 1 \quad \text{and} \quad L = \frac{n+1}{2}$$

### Theorem 4 — Maximum Leaves at a Given Height
A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^{h}$ leaves. Conversely, the minimum height of a binary tree with $L$ leaves is $\lceil \log_{2} L \rceil$.

### Theorem 5 — Maximum Number of Vertices at Level k
At level $k$ (root at level 0), a binary tree has at most $2^{k}$ vertices.

### Theorem 6 — Total Vertex Count
A binary tree of height $h$ contains at most $2^{h+1} - 1$ vertices.

### Theorem 7 — Skewed Tree (Worst Case)
A skewed binary tree (degenerate tree) of height $h$ contains $h+1$ vertices. This is the **minimum** number of vertices for a given height.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete formula dossier** required for KTU Part B questions on Rooted and Binary Trees.

| # | Property | Formula | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Edges in any tree with $n$ vertices | $e = n - 1$ | All trees |
| 2 | Leaves in a full binary tree with $i$ internal nodes | $L = i + 1$ | Full binary trees |
| 3 | Total vertices in a full binary tree | $n = 2i + 1$ | Full binary trees |
| 4 | Leaves when total vertices $= n$ (full tree) | $L = \frac{n+1}{2}$ | Full binary trees |
| 5 | Max leaves at height $h$ | $L_{\max} = 2^{h}$ | Any binary tree |
| 6 | Max vertices at level $k$ | $V_{k} = 2^{k}$ | Any binary tree |
| 7 | Max total vertices of height $h$ | $n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Any binary tree |
| 8 | Min height with $n$ vertices | $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_{2} n \rfloor$ | Any binary tree |
| 9 | Min height with $L$ leaves | $h_{\min} = \lceil \log_{2} L \rceil$ | Any binary tree |
| 10 | Catalan number — binary trees with $n$ internal nodes | $C_{n} = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$ | Counting |
| 11 | Catalan number recurrence | $C_{n+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} C_{i} C_{n-i}$ | Counting |
| 12 | Fibonacci relation for full binary trees | $f_{n} = f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}$ | Recurrence |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ in $\vert x \vert$ is replaced above by $\mid$ to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Construction:** Expression trees (a binary tree subtype) are parsed by every modern compiler (GCC, Clang) to convert infix arithmetic into optimized machine code.
- **Network Routing:** Tree-structured routing tables (tries / radix trees) accelerate IP lookups from $O(n)$ to $O(\log n)$.
- **Database Indexing:** B-trees (generalized balanced trees) underpin every relational database index.
- **AI/Decision Systems:** Decision trees in machine learning (Random Forests, XGBoost) are rooted binary trees.
- **Bioinformatics:** Phylogenetic trees (rooted, often binary) reconstruct evolutionary history from genetic data.

## 2.5 Traversal Orders (Mandatory for KTU)

For any binary tree, there are **four canonical traversals**:

| Traversal | Order of Visiting Root | Recursive Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Preorder** (Root–Left–Right) | Visit $v$ first | Pre($v$): print $v$; Pre($L$); Pre($R$) |
| **Inorder** (Left–Root–Right) | Visit $v$ between subtrees | In($v$): In($L$); print $v$; In($R$) |
| **Postorder** (Left–Right–Root) | Visit $v$ last | Post($v$): Post($L$); Post($R$); print $v$) |
| **Level-order** (BFS) | Visit level by level | Uses a queue |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory mnemonic:** **P**reorder = **P**arent first. Postorder = **P**ostpone parent to the end. Inorder = **In**-between the two children.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving Leaves = Internal Nodes + 1 in a Full Binary Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** A full binary tree $T$ with $i$ internal vertices and $L$ leaves.
> **To Prove:** $L = i + 1$.

### Step-by-Step Proof (Every Transition Shown):

**Step 1 — Count edges using vertices.**
Every tree with $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges. Here $n = i + L$, so:
$$e = (i + L) - 1$$

**Step 2 — Count edges using children (out-degrees).**
In a full binary tree, every internal vertex has **exactly 2 children**, so the sum of out-degrees over all internal vertices is $2i$. Leaves have out-degree $0$. Hence:
$$e = 2i$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $e$.**
$$2i = (i + L) - 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L$.**
$$2i - i + 1 = L \implies L = i + 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Equivalently express in terms of total vertices $n = i + L$.**
Substituting $L = i + 1$:
$$n = i + (i + 1) = 2i + 1 \implies i = \frac{n - 1}{2}, \quad L = \frac{n + 1}{2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Vertices in a Binary Tree of Height $h$

We proceed by **mathematical induction on the height $h$**.

### Base Case ($h = 0$):
A binary tree of height $0$ consists of a single root node. Hence:
$$V_{\max}(0) = 1 = 2^{0+1} - 1 = 2^{1} - 1 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Inductive Step:
**Assume** the result holds for height $h - 1$:
$$V_{\max}(h - 1) = 2^{h} - 1$$

For a tree of height $h$, the root has two subtrees, each of height **at most $h - 1$**. By the induction hypothesis, the maximum number of vertices in each subtree is $2^{h} - 1$. Adding the root itself:
$$V_{\max}(h) = 1 + 2 \cdot (2^{h} - 1) = 1 + 2^{h+1} - 2 = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

Hence proved by induction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Catalan Numbers for Counting Binary Trees

The number of **distinct binary trees with $n$ internal nodes** (equivalently $n+1$ leaves in the full case) is the $n$-th Catalan number $C_{n}$.

### Derivation via the Recurrence:

**Step 1 — Root has $k$ nodes on the left.**
In any binary tree, fix the number of internal nodes in the **left subtree** as $k$, where $0 \leq k \leq n-1$. Then the right subtree contains $n - 1 - k$ internal nodes.

**Step 2 — Count choices.**
- Number of ways to build the left subtree: $C_{k}$.
- Number of ways to build the right subtree: $C_{n-1-k}$.

**Step 3 — Sum over all possible $k$.**
$$C_{n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} C_{k} \, C_{n-1-k}$$

**Step 4 — Closed form using binomial coefficients.**
By combinatorial argument or generating functions:
$$C_{n} = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$$

### First few Catalan values (KTU Table — memorize this!):

| $n$ | $C_{n}$ | Tree Count |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 | 2 |
| 3 | 5 | 5 |
| 4 | 14 | 14 |
| 5 | 42 | 42 |

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fibonacci Recurrence for Full Binary Trees

> [!NOTE]
> The number of full binary trees with $n$ internal nodes satisfies the Fibonacci-like recurrence:
> $$f_{n} = f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}, \quad f_{0} = 1, \quad f_{1} = 1$$

**Why?** In any full binary tree:
- The root's left subtree has $k$ internal nodes and the right subtree has $n-1-k$ nodes, for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1$.
- For each split $(k, n-1-k)$, the total number of full binary trees is the product $f_{k} \cdot f_{n-1-k}$.
- Summing over all $k$ gives the Catalan recurrence.
- A clever combinatorial bijection maps full binary trees with $n$ internal nodes to subsets of an $(n-1)$-element set, giving the Fibonacci count.

**Worked example (height 3):** The number of full binary trees with 3 internal nodes is $f_{3} = f_{2} + f_{1} = 2 + 1 = 3$. (Visually verifiable: there are exactly 3 such trees up to isomorphism.)

## 3.5 Worked Problem — Traversal Output

Consider the binary tree:
```
              [50]
             /    \
          [30]    [70]
         /   \    /  \
       [20] [40][60] [80]
```

**Preorder** (Root, Left, Right): 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80

**Inorder** (Left, Root, Right): 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80  *(yields sorted order for a BST!)*

**Postorder** (Left, Right, Root): 20, 40, 30, 60, 80, 70, 50

**Level-order** (BFS): 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80

> [!TIP]
> **Verification check:** In a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, inorder traversal **always** produces a sorted sequence. This is a common 3-mark KTU sub-question.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Generic KTU Problem

| Given Quantity | What to Find | Formula to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ (total vertices) | $e$ (edges) | $e = n - 1$ |
| $L$ (leaves) | $i$ (internal nodes), full tree | $i = L - 1$ |
| $i$ (internal nodes) | $L$ (leaves), full tree | $L = i + 1$ |
| $h$ (height) | $n_{\max}$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ |
| $n$ (vertices) | $h_{\min}$ | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ |
| $L$ (leaves) | $h_{\min}$ | $\lceil \log_2 L \rceil$ |
| $n$ (internal nodes) | Number of tree structures | Catalan $C_n$ |
| Traversal needed | BST sorted output | Inorder traversal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Sample Binary Tree with Node Labels

> The diagram below is rendered using **Mermaid**. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase text per the engine's safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA(("A - root"))
    nodeB(("B"))
    nodeC(("C"))
    nodeD(("D - leaf"))
    nodeE(("E"))
    nodeF(("F - leaf"))
    nodeG(("G - leaf"))
    nodeH(("H - leaf"))
    nodeI(("I - leaf"))

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeE --- nodeG
    nodeC --- nodeH
    nodeC --- nodeI
```

**Reading the diagram:** The root `nodeA` has two children `nodeB` and `nodeC`. The leaves (no children) are `nodeD, nodeF, nodeG, nodeH, nodeI`. The internal node is `nodeE`. Tree height = 3 (longest path A→C→I).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture for Tree Traversal

> This block diagram maps the **algorithmic flow** of a recursive inorder traversal, which is a common KTU Part B topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode(["START: invoke INORDER root"])
    checkRoot{"Is root NULL?"}
    recurseLeft["Recurse: INORDER root LEFT"]
    visitRoot["PRINT root VALUE"]
    recurseRight["Recurse: INORDER root RIGHT"]
    returnNode(["RETURN to caller"])

    startNode --> checkRoot
    checkRoot -- YES --> returnNode
    checkRoot -- NO --> recurseLeft
    recurseLeft --> visitRoot
    visitRoot --> recurseRight
    recurseRight --> returnNode
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Binary Tree Types

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T1["FULL BINARY TREE"]
        fa(("A")) --- fb(("B"))
        fa --- fc(("C"))
        fb --- fd(("D"))
        fb --- fe(("E"))
        fc --- ff(("F"))
        fc --- fg(("G"))
    end

    subgraph T2["SKEWED LEFT BINARY TREE"]
        sa(("A")) --- sb(("B"))
        sb --- sc(("C"))
        sc --- sd(("D"))
    end

    subgraph T3["PERFECT BINARY TREE"]
        pa(("A")) --- pb(("B"))
        pa --- pc(("C"))
        pb --- pd(("D"))
        pb --- pe(("E"))
        pc --- pf(("F"))
        pc --- pg(("G"))
    end
```

**Comparative reading:**
- **Full tree** (T1): every internal node has 2 children, but leaves may be at varying depths.
- **Skewed tree** (T2): degenerate, every node has only one left child — the **worst case** for search operations ($O(n)$).
- **Perfect tree** (T3): all leaves at the **same level** — the **best case** for search operations ($O(\log n)$).

## 4.4 Schematic — Expression Tree for $(a + b) \cdot (c - d/e)$

```
              [*]
            /     \
          [+]     [-]
         /   \   /   \
        a     b c    [/]
                  /   \
                 d     e
```

**Infix reconstruction** (Inorder traversal): $a + b \cdot c - d / e$ ... actually, with proper tree evaluation, the expression evaluates to $(a+b) \cdot (c - d/e)$ via postorder traversal.

**Postorder** (used by compilers to generate stack-based code): $a \; b \; + \; c \; d \; e \; / \; - \; *$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a rooted tree and a binary tree. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rooted tree** is a connected acyclic graph in which one vertex is designated as the **root** and every edge is directed away from the root.

> Each vertex (except the root) has exactly one parent, and there is a unique path from the root to every other vertex.

**Example:** A company's organizational chart (CEO at top, employees below in a hierarchy).

A **binary tree** is a rooted tree in which every vertex has **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.

**Example:** A Binary Search Tree (BST) used for dictionary lookup, where left subtree keys are smaller and right subtree keys are larger.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the theorem: "A full binary tree with $i$ internal vertices has $L = i + 1$ leaves."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise proof):**

**Statement:** In a full binary tree, if $i$ is the number of internal vertices and $L$ the number of leaves, then $L = i + 1$.

**Proof:** Let $n$ be the total vertices, so $n = i + L$. The number of edges is $e = n - 1 = i + L - 1$.

Since every internal vertex has exactly 2 children, the sum of out-degrees of internal vertices is $2i$, and leaves have out-degree 0, so $e = 2i$.

Equating: $2i = i + L - 1 \implies L = i + 1$. $\blacksquare$

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Define a binary tree. For the binary tree given below, find the **inorder, preorder, and postorder** traversals. *(7 marks)*

```
              [10]
             /    \
          [5]     [15]
         /   \       \
      [3]   [7]     [20]
              \     /
              [8] [18]
```

**(b)** A full binary tree has **10 internal vertices**. Find: (i) the number of leaves, (ii) the number of edges, (iii) the total number of vertices, (iv) the maximum height possible. *(7 marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Traversals (7 marks)

**Preorder (Root, Left, Right):**
- Visit root: 10
- Preorder of left subtree [5]: 5, 3, 7, 8
- Preorder of right subtree [15]: 15, 20, 18

**Preorder sequence:** $\boxed{10, \; 5, \; 3, \; 7, \; 8, \; 15, \; 20, \; 18}$

**Inorder (Left, Root, Right):**
- Inorder of left subtree: 3, 5, 7, 8
- Root: 10
- Inorder of right subtree: 15, 18, 20

**Inorder sequence:** $\boxed{3, \; 5, \; 7, \; 8, \; 10, \; 15, \; 18, \; 20}$

**Postorder (Left, Right, Root):**
- Postorder of left subtree: 3, 8, 7, 5
- Postorder of right subtree: 18, 20, 15
- Root last: 10

**Postorder sequence:** $\boxed{3, \; 8, \; 7, \; 5, \; 18, \; 20, \; 15, \; 10}$

#### Part (b) — Full Binary Tree with $i = 10$ Internal Vertices (7 marks)

**(i) Number of leaves** using $L = i + 1$:
$$L = 10 + 1 = 11 \text{ leaves}$$

**(ii) Number of edges** using $e = 2i$ (full binary tree):
$$e = 2(10) = 20 \text{ edges}$$

**(iii) Total vertices:**
$$n = i + L = 10 + 11 = 21 \text{ vertices}$$

*Verification:* $e = n - 1 = 20 \checkmark$

**(iv) Maximum height** — for a full binary tree with 11 leaves, $h_{\max}$ corresponds to the **skewed case** where height equals the number of internal nodes along a path. Since a full binary tree is a special structure, the maximum height is bounded:
$$h_{\max} = i = 10 \quad \text{(when the tree degenerates to a path)}$$

The **minimum height** (most balanced):
$$h_{\min} = \lceil \log_2 11 \rceil = \lceil 3.459 \rceil = 4$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert:**
> - Many students confuse "maximum height" with "minimum height" in full binary tree problems. **Maximum height** = degenerate (path-like) case, $h = i$. **Minimum height** = perfectly balanced, $h = \lceil \log_2(i+1) \rceil$. Losing 2 marks here is very common.
> - In traversal questions, students often forget to apply recursion properly. Always state the recursive order: "Root first (pre), root in middle (in), root last (post)."

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Define a **full binary tree**, **complete binary tree**, and **perfect binary tree** with diagrams. State **two** properties of binary trees. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A binary tree has **height $h = 4$**. Find:
  (i) The maximum number of leaves possible.
  (ii) The maximum number of vertices.
  (iii) The minimum number of vertices.
  (iv) The maximum number of vertices at level 3. *(7 marks)*

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Properties (7 marks)

**Full Binary Tree:** Every internal vertex has **exactly 2 children** (and no internal vertex has 1 child).

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \
    D   E
```

**Complete Binary Tree:** All levels are completely filled **except possibly the last**, and the last level is filled **strictly from left to right** (no gaps).

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \  /
    D  E F
```

**Perfect Binary Tree:** All internal vertices have exactly 2 children **and** all leaves lie at the **same level**.

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \ / \
    D  E F  G
```

**Two properties of binary trees:**
1. A binary tree with $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges.
2. A binary tree of height $h$ contains at most $2^{h+1} - 1$ vertices.
3. The maximum number of leaves is $2^{h}$, and the minimum height with $L$ leaves is $\lceil \log_2 L \rceil$.

#### Part (b) — Binary Tree with $h = 4$ (7 marks)

**(i) Maximum number of leaves:**
$$L_{\max} = 2^{h} = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ leaves}$$

**(ii) Maximum number of vertices:**
$$n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1 = 2^{5} - 1 = 32 - 1 = 31 \text{ vertices}$$

**(iii) Minimum number of vertices:**
This occurs in the **skewed (degenerate)** case:
$$n_{\min} = h + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ vertices}$$

**(iv) Maximum number of vertices at level 3** (root at level 0):
$$V_{\max}(\ell = 3) = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ vertices}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often confuse "height" with "level number." Remember: **level of root = 0**, so a tree of height $h$ has levels $0, 1, \ldots, h$.
> - In the **minimum vertices** sub-question, do not write 1 (that would mean a tree of height 0, not 4). The minimum is $h+1$ for a *degenerate* path-shaped tree.
> - The phrase "maximum height" is ambiguous if not specified — always check if the problem asks for the **balanced** or **skewed** extreme.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (memorize before the exam):**

- **Rooted Tree** = tree with a designated root; all edges directed away from root. Every non-root vertex has **exactly one parent**.
- **Binary Tree** = rooted tree where every vertex has **at most 2 children** (left and right are distinguishable).
- **Full binary tree property:** $L = i + 1$ and $n = 2i + 1$.
- **Edge count in any tree:** $e = n - 1$.
- **Maximum leaves at height $h$:** $L_{\max} = 2^{h}$.
- **Maximum vertices at height $h$:** $n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$.
- **Minimum vertices at height $h$** (skewed case): $n_{\min} = h + 1$.
- **Maximum vertices at level $k$:** $V_{k} = 2^{k}$.
- **Minimum height with $n$ vertices:** $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.
- **Minimum height with $L$ leaves:** $h_{\min} = \lceil \log_2 L \rceil$.
- **Preorder** = Root, Left, Right (used to **copy** a tree).
- **Inorder** = Left, Root, Right (BSTs yield **sorted output**).
- **Postorder** = Left, Right, Root (used to **delete** a tree; compilers use it for postfix evaluation).
- **Level-order** = BFS using a queue.
- **Counting binary trees with $n$ internal nodes** = Catalan number $C_n = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$.
- **Counting full binary trees** = satisfies Fibonacci recurrence $f_n = f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}$ with $f_0 = f_1 = 1$.
- **Ancillary facts:** A tree is connected and acyclic. Adding any edge creates exactly one cycle.
- **Level numbering starts at 0** at the root (KTU convention).
- **Expression trees** use postorder to evaluate; use inorder to recover infix (with parentheses).
- **KTU must-know Catalan values:** $C_0=1, C_1=1, C_2=2, C_3=5, C_4=14, C_5=42$.
- **Memory trick for L vs. R in BST:** "**L**eft is **L**esser, **R**ight is **R**ight (greater)".

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> 1. For **traversal questions**, always write the recursive definition before tracing the path — examiners award partial credit for correct methodology.
> 2. For **counting questions**, explicitly state the formula you are using, then substitute the value. Don't skip the formula citation.
> 3. For **proofs**, label every step with a justification (e.g., "by the induction hypothesis," "since $e = n - 1$").
> 4. Draw the tree cleanly in traversal problems — sloppy diagrams lead to transcription errors and lost marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counting Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science 4
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Counting Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌳 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Tree

A **tree** is a connected, undirected graph that contains **no cycles**. For a tree $T$ with $n$ vertices, the following equivalent characterizations hold:

1. $T$ is connected and has exactly $n - 1$ edges.
2. $T$ is acyclic and has exactly $n - 1$ edges.
3. $T$ is connected and the removal of any single edge disconnects it.
4. There exists exactly one simple path between every pair of vertices in $T$.

A **labeled tree** is a tree whose vertices are assigned distinct labels (typically drawn from the set $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, n\}$). The labels matter — two trees that look structurally identical but have different vertex labelings are considered *different* labeled trees.

A **spanning tree** of a connected graph $G$ is a subgraph of $G$ that is a tree and contains every vertex of $G$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a tree like a family genealogy chart or a corporate reporting hierarchy.**

- A **leaf** is a vertex with degree 1 (a person with no children / an intern with no reports).
- A **root** (in rooted trees) is a designated "ancestor" or "CEO" — though in our module we deal with **free (unrooted) labeled trees**.
- The total number of edges is always one less than the number of vertices because a tree has **no redundant loops** — just the minimum scaffolding needed to keep everything connected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The central counting problem of this module is:** *"How many different labeled trees can be built using $n$ distinct vertices?"* This is one of the most elegant results in discrete mathematics, and the answer turns out to be a beautifully simple formula: $n^{n-2}$.

## 1.3 Engineering & Computer Science Significance

Counting trees is foundational to:
- **Network design** — minimum-cost backbone connections (telecoms, LANs).
- **Phylogenetic trees** in computational biology.
- **Decision tree complexity** in machine learning.
- **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** algorithms such as Kruskal's and Prim's.
- **Reliability analysis** — number of alternative connection paths in a network.

The two principal counting tools we will master are:
1. **Cayley's Formula** combined with **Prüfer Sequences** (combinatorial bijection).
2. **Kirchhoff's Matrix-Tree Theorem** (algebraic / linear-algebraic method).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Properties of a Tree

Let $T = (V, E)$ be a tree with $\vert V \vert = n$.

**Property 1 — Edge Count:** $\vert E \vert = n - 1$.

**Property 2 — Acyclicity:** $T$ has no cycles.

**Property 3 — Connectivity:** $T$ is connected.

**Property 4 — Uniqueness of Path:** For any two vertices $u, v \in V$, there is exactly one simple path joining them.

**Property 5 — Leaf Existence:** Every tree with $n \geq 2$ has at least two leaves (vertices of degree 1).

## 2.2 The Counting Problem Statement

> **Problem:** Given a complete graph $K_n$ on $n$ labeled vertices, how many **distinct spanning trees** does it possess? Equivalently, how many labeled trees exist on $n$ vertices?

A spanning tree of $K_n$ uses all $n$ vertices and only $n - 1$ of the $\binom{n}{2}$ available edges. Two labeled trees are *distinct* if their edge sets differ, even if they have the same shape (the **shape** is called the *unlabeled* or *non-isomorphic* tree count, which is a different, harder problem).

## 2.3 The Master Formula: Cayley's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cayley's Theorem (1889):** The number of distinct labeled trees on $n$ vertices is $n^{\,n-2}$.

This is also equal to the number of spanning trees of the complete graph $K_n$, denoted $\tau(K_n)$.

| $n$ | $n^{n-2}$ | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $1^{-1} = 1$ | Single isolated vertex |
| 2 | $2^{0} = 1$ | Only one edge joining them |
| 3 | $3^{1} = 3$ | Three possible "star" paths |
| 4 | $4^{2} = 16$ | All spanning trees of $K_4$ |

## 2.4 The Prüfer Sequence — A Bijective Bridge

The proof of Cayley's formula is constructive. **Heinz Prüfer (1918)** established a one-to-one correspondence between:

$$\{\text{Labeled trees on } \{1, 2, \dots, n\}\} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \{1, 2, \dots, n\}^{n-2}$$

Here, the right side is the set of all sequences of length $n - 2$ whose entries come from $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$. The number of such sequences is exactly $n^{n-2}$.

### 2.4.1 Encoding Algorithm (Tree → Sequence)

Given a labeled tree $T$ on vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$:

1. Initialise an empty list $S$.
2. Find the **smallest labeled leaf** (vertex of degree 1).
3. Append the label of its unique neighbor to $S$.
4. Delete the leaf and its incident edge from $T$.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until only 2 vertices remain.
6. Output the list $S$ — it has length $n - 2$.

### 2.4.2 Decoding Algorithm (Sequence → Tree)

Given a Prüfer sequence $S = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n-2})$ of labels from $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$:

1. Compute the **degree** of each vertex: $\deg(v) = 1 + (\text{count of } v \text{ in } S)$.
2. Initialise edge set $E = \emptyset$.
3. For $i = 1$ to $n - 2$:
   - Let $\ell$ be the **smallest** vertex whose current degree is $1$.
   - Add the edge $\{\ell, a_i\}$ to $E$.
   - Decrease $\deg(\ell)$ by 1 and $\deg(a_i)$ by 1.
4. Add the final edge connecting the two remaining vertices of degree 1.
5. Output $(V, E)$.

## 2.5 Kirchhoff's Matrix-Tree Theorem

For a general connected graph $G$ (not necessarily complete), the number of spanning trees $\tau(G)$ can be computed using the **Laplacian matrix** $L$ of $G$:

$$L_{ij} = \begin{cases} \deg(v_i) & \text{if } i = j \\ -1 & \text{if } i \neq j \text{ and } \{v_i, v_j\} \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kirchhoff's Matrix-Tree Theorem:** $\tau(G)$ equals **any cofactor** of the Laplacian matrix $L$. Equivalently, delete any one row and the corresponding column of $L$, then compute the determinant of the resulting $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ matrix.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| \# | Formula / Rule | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | $\tau(K_n) = n^{n-2}$ | Cayley's count of labeled trees / spanning trees of $K_n$ |
| F2 | $\vert E \vert = n - 1$ | A tree on $n$ vertices always has $n-1$ edges |
| F3 | Prüfer length $= n - 2$ | Bijection length |
| F4 | $\deg(v) = 1 + c_S(v)$ | Degree from sequence count |
| F5 | $\tau(G) = \det(L_{ii})$ | Matrix-tree theorem (any cofactor) |
| F6 | $\sum_v \deg(v) = 2(n-1)$ | Handshaking lemma for trees |
| F7 | Leaves of $T \geq 2$ | Every $n \geq 2$ tree has at least 2 leaves |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of Cayley's Theorem via Prüfer Bijection

We prove the count by exhibiting a **bijection** between the set $\mathcal{T}_n$ of labeled trees on $\{1, \dots, n\}$ and the set $\mathcal{S}_n$ of all sequences of length $n - 2$ with entries from $\{1, \dots, n\}$.

### Step 1 — Injectivity (Different trees give different sequences)

Suppose two distinct trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ yield the same Prüfer sequence $S$. Both trees contain exactly the same edges that were added during decoding (since decoding is deterministic from $S$ and the initial degree multiset). This forces $T_1 = T_2$, a contradiction. Hence the encoding map is **injective**.

### Step 2 — Surjectivity (Every sequence comes from some tree)

Given any sequence $S = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n-2})$, the decoding algorithm uniquely produces an edge set $E$. The output has exactly $n - 1$ edges and is connected by construction, so it is a tree. Hence the encoding is **surjective**.

### Step 3 — Counting

Since the map is a bijection:

$$\vert \mathcal{T}_n \vert = \vert \mathcal{S}_n \vert = n^{n-2}$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example 1 — Encoding a Tree to Prüfer Sequence

**Tree $T$** on vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ with edges:

$$\{(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (2,5),\ (5,6)\}$$

We apply the encoding algorithm step by step.

| Step | Leaves Present | Smallest Leaf | Its Neighbour | Output Sequence | Remaining Vertices |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3, 4, 6 | 3 | 1 | $(1)$ | $\{1,2,4,5,6\}$ |
| 2 | 4, 6 | 4 | 2 | $(1,2)$ | $\{1,2,5,6\}$ |
| 3 | 1, 6 | 1 | 2 | $(1,2,2)$ | $\{2,5,6\}$ |
| 4 | 6 | 6 | 5 | $(1,2,2,5)$ | $\{2,5\}$ |

The resulting Prüfer sequence is:

$$S = (1,\ 2,\ 2,\ 5)$$

Note the length: $n - 2 = 6 - 2 = 4$. ✓

**Verification of vertex degrees from the sequence:**

$$\deg_T(1) = 1 + c_S(1) = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$\deg_T(2) = 1 + c_S(2) = 1 + 2 = 3$$
$$\deg_T(3) = 1 + c_S(3) = 1 + 0 = 1$$
$$\deg_T(4) = 1 + c_S(4) = 1 + 0 = 1$$
$$\deg_T(5) = 1 + c_S(5) = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$\deg_T(6) = 1 + c_S(6) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Sum check:** $2 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 10 = 2(n-1) = 2(5)$ ✓

These match the original tree: 1 connects to 2, 3 (deg 2); 2 connects to 1, 4, 5 (deg 3); 5 connects to 2, 6 (deg 2).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example 2 — Decoding a Prüfer Sequence to a Tree

**Given sequence:** $S = (1, 2, 2, 5)$ on $n = 6$ vertices.

**Step 0 — Compute initial degrees:**

| Vertex $v$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\deg(v) = 1 + c_S(v)$ | 2 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 |

**Step 1 — Iteration $i = 1$:** $a_1 = 1$. Smallest leaf is $3$ (deg 1). Add edge $\{3, 1\}$. Update $\deg(3) \to 0$, $\deg(1) \to 1$.

**Step 2 — Iteration $i = 2$:** $a_2 = 2$. Smallest leaf is $4$ (deg 1). Add edge $\{4, 2\}$. Update $\deg(4) \to 0$, $\deg(2) \to 2$.

**Step 3 — Iteration $i = 3$:** $a_3 = 2$. Smallest leaf is $1$ (deg 1). Add edge $\{1, 2\}$. Update $\deg(1) \to 0$, $\deg(2) \to 1$.

**Step 4 — Iteration $i = 4$:** $a_4 = 5$. Smallest leaf is $6$ (deg 1). Add edge $\{6, 5\}$. Update $\deg(6) \to 0$, $\deg(5) \to 1$.

**Step 5 — Final step:** Two remaining vertices of degree 1 are $2$ and $5$. Add edge $\{2, 5\}$.

**Reconstructed edge set:**

$$E = \bigl\{(1,3),\ (2,4),\ (1,2),\ (5,6),\ (2,5)\bigr\}$$

This is exactly the original tree — bijection confirmed. ✓

---

## 3.4 Application — Counting Sub-Trees in a Computer Network

**Problem:** A LAN has 5 computers (labeled 1–5) that must be connected in a tree topology. Each pair *can* be directly connected, but the network engineer only wants to know how many possible topologies exist.

**Solution:** Apply Cayley's formula directly with $n = 5$:

$$\tau(K_5) = 5^{5-2} = 5^3 = 125 \text{ distinct labeled trees}$$

If the engineer also wants the count of distinct *network shapes* (ignoring labels), the count is much smaller (only 3 non-isomorphic trees on 5 vertices), but the question of *labeled* connection patterns yields 125.

---

## 3.5 Application — Counting Spanning Trees via Laplacian

**Graph $G$:** A cycle $C_4$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,1)\}$.

**Laplacian matrix:**

$$L = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Delete the last row and last column:

$$L_{44} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute the determinant by cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$\det(L_{44}) = 2 \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - (-1) \begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{vmatrix} + 0$$

$$= 2(4 - 1) + 1(-2 - 0) = 2(3) - 2 = 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Result:** $\tau(C_4) = 4$ spanning trees, which matches the obvious count (4 ways to remove exactly one edge from the 4-cycle to break it into a tree).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow: The Tree–Sequence–Tree Pipeline

The Prüfer bijection is best understood as a two-way transformation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputT["Labeled Tree T on n vertices"]
    inputS["Pruefer Sequence S of length n-2"]
    outputT["Reconstructed Labeled Tree T'"]
    outputS["Encoded Sequence S'"]
    encode["Encoding Algorithm: Repeatedly remove smallest leaf and record its neighbor"]
    decode["Decoding Algorithm: Reconstruct degrees and pair smallest leaf with next sequence element"]

    inputT --> encode
    encode --> outputS
    inputS --> decode
    decode --> outputT

    outputS -. Same length n-2 .-> inputS
    outputT -. Equals original T by bijection .-> inputT
```

## 4.2 Schematic: All 16 Labeled Trees on 4 Vertices ($\tau(K_4) = 4^2 = 16$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    style1["Star K_{1,3}: 12 trees"]
    style2["Path P_4: 12 trees"]
    style3["Wait, 12 plus 12 equals 24, not 16"]
    style_final["Correction: Stars (degree sequence 3,1,1,1) contribute 4 labeled trees; Paths contribute 12 labeled trees. Total: 4 + 12 = 16"]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef star fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    star1["v1 -- v2, v1 -- v3, v1 -- v4 (center 1)"]:::star
    star2["v2 -- v1, v2 -- v3, v2 -- v4 (center 2)"]:::star
    star3["v3 -- v1, v3 -- v2, v3 -- v4 (center 3)"]:::star
    star4["v4 -- v1, v4 -- v2, v4 -- v3 (center 4)"]:::star

    path1["1-2-3-4"]:::path
    path2["1-2-4-3"]:::path
    path3["1-3-2-4"]:::path
    path4["1-3-4-2"]:::path
    path5["1-4-2-3"]:::path
    path6["1-4-3-2"]:::path
    path7["2-1-3-4"]:::path
    path8["2-1-4-3"]:::path
    path9["3-1-2-4"]:::path
    path10["3-1-4-2"]:::path
    path11["4-1-2-3"]:::path
    path12["4-1-3-2"]:::path

    group1["4 STAR TREES (one per choice of center)"]:::star
    group2["12 PATH TREES (one per permutation not symmetric to stars)"]:::path

    group1 --> star1
    group1 --> star2
    group1 --> star3
    group1 --> star4

    group2 --> path1
    group2 --> path2
    group2 --> path3
    group2 --> path4
    group2 --> path5
    group2 --> path6
    group2 --> path7
    group2 --> path8
