> 5. **Not showing intermediate tables** — KTU board examiners award marks for *clean tabular presentation*. A messy scratch work loses presentation marks even if the answer is correct.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm**

- **Type:** Greedy, single-source, non-negative-weight shortest-path algorithm.
- **Pre-condition:** All edge weights must satisfy $w(u, v) \geq 0$. For negative weights, use **Bellman-Ford**.
- **Key data structures:** `dist[]` array, `prev[]` array, `visited[]` flag (or min-priority queue `Q`).
- **Initialization:** $dist[s] = 0$, $dist[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$.
- **Selection rule:** At each step, pick $u$ with the **minimum tentative distance** among unvisited vertices.
- **Core operation:** **Edge relaxation** — if $dist[u] + w(u, v) < dist[v]$, then update $dist[v]$ and $prev[v]$.
- **Termination:** When all vertices are visited, or when the next minimum tentative distance is $\infty$ (unreachable).
- **Path reconstruction:** Back-trace $T \gets prev[T] \gets prev[prev[T]] \gets \cdots \gets s$, then reverse the sequence.
- **Time complexity (binary heap):** $O((n + m) \log n)$, where $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$.
- **Time complexity (array):** $O(n^2)$ — better for dense graphs.
- **Time complexity (Fibonacci heap):** $O(n \log n + m)$ — theoretically optimal.
- **Space complexity:** $O(n + m)$ for the graph, $O(n)$ for auxiliary arrays.
- **Output:** A shortest-path tree rooted at $s$, with $n - 1$ edges.
- **Common application:** Network routing protocols (OSPF), GPS navigation, AI pathfinding.
- **Failure mode:** Any negative edge weight breaks the greedy optimality — switch to **Bellman-Ford**.
- **Exam tip:** Always present the iteration in a **tabular format**; KTU examiners reward clean tables with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floyd-Warshall Shortest Path Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science  4
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floyd-Warshall Shortest Path Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Floyd-Warshall Algorithm** is a classic **Dynamic Programming** algorithm used to find the **shortest paths between all pairs of vertices** in a weighted graph (directed or undirected) with positive or negative edge weights (but no negative cycles). It operates in a time complexity of **O(V³)**, where **V** is the number of vertices, by progressively improving an estimate on the shortest path between two vertices using an intermediate vertex.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine you are a travel agent who must find the **shortest possible route between *every* pair of cities** in a country. Instead of solving each query separately, you build a giant flight network matrix. You start by saying "The shortest distance from A to B is the direct flight." Then you ask: *"Is there a shorter route if I allow one stopover at city C?"* You check this for **all intermediate cities, one at a time**. After checking all cities as potential stopovers, your matrix is guaranteed to hold the absolute shortest distances between **every single pair of cities** simultaneously.

### Key Standard Metrics in Bold
- **Time Complexity:** **O(V³)**
- **Space Complexity:** **O(V²)**
- **Vertices (V):** Number of nodes in the graph
- **Edges (E):** Number of connections in the graph
- **Weight Matrix W[i][j]:** The initial edge weight between vertex *i* and *j*
- **Distance Matrix D[i][j]:** The shortest path distance between vertex *i* and *j*

### Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)
- **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP):** Finding shortest paths for every vertex-to-vertex combination
- **Transitive Closure:** A related concept where we check reachability (1 if reachable, 0 if not)
- **Intermediate Vertex:** A vertex allowed to lie on the path between *i* and *j*
- **Negative Cycle:** A cycle whose total edge weight is negative (Floyd-Warshall can detect these)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Core Dynamic Programming Idea
Floyd-Warshall is based on a remarkably elegant recursive insight. The shortest path from vertex *i* to vertex *j* either:
1. Does **NOT** pass through intermediate vertex *k* (uses previous best estimate), **OR**
2. **DOES** pass through vertex *k*, in which case it splits into a path from *i → k* followed by a path from *k → j*

### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1: Initialize** the distance matrix **D⁰** with direct edge weights from the weighted adjacency matrix **W**. If no edge exists, the weight is **∞**. Self-loops are set to **0**.
- **Step 2: Iterate** for each intermediate vertex *k* from **1 to V**.
- **Step 3: Update** every pair *(i, j)* using the **relaxation formula** considering vertex *k* as an allowed stopover.
- **Step 4: Conclude** that after *k = V* iterations, **D^V[i][j]** holds the shortest path from *i* to *j*.
- **Step 5: Detect** negative cycles by checking if any diagonal element **D[i][i] < 0**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initialization** | $D^{(0)}[i][j] = W[i][j]$ | $W[i][i] = 0$ and $W[i][j] = \infty$ if no direct edge |
| **Recurrence Relation** | $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ | The heart of the algorithm |
| **Negative Cycle Test** | If $\exists \; i : D[i][i] < 0$ | Graph contains a negative cycle |
| **Path Reconstruction** | $\Pi[i][j] = k$ if path goes through *k* | Using a predecessor matrix |
| **Transitive Closure** | $T^{(k)}[i][j] = T^{(k-1)}[i][j] \lor (T^{(k-1)}[i][k] \land T^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ | Boolean version of Floyd-Warshall |
| **Final Result** | $D^{(V)}[i][j]$ | True shortest path from *i* to *j* |
| **Time Complexity** | $\Theta(V^3)$ | Three nested loops over V |
| **Space Complexity** | $\Theta(V^2)$ | In-place update is possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Recurrence Relation Insight:** The formula $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ is a hallmark of dynamic programming. The "smaller subproblem" is the shortest path that **already considered** vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, k-1\}$ as intermediates.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **GPS and Navigation Systems:** Computing optimal multi-stop delivery routes
- **Network Routing Protocols:** Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) uses link-state algorithms similar in spirit
- **Database Query Optimization:** Finding optimal join orders in distributed systems
- **Telecommunications:** Analyzing routing paths in mesh networks
- **Game Development:** AI pathfinding in tile-based maps
- **Airline/Logistics:** Hub-and-spoke shortest route calculations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Example)

### Problem Statement
Consider a directed weighted graph with **V = 4** vertices. The initial weight matrix **W** (where **∞** denotes no direct edge) is given below. Apply the **Floyd-Warshall algorithm** to determine the all-pairs shortest path matrix.

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\
5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
2 & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1: Initialization ($D^{(0)}$)
Set $D^{(0)} = W$. This represents the shortest path from *i* to *j* using **no intermediate vertices**.

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\
5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
2 & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** $D^{(0)}[i][j]$ is just the direct edge weight, with self-loops zero and missing edges set to infinity.

---

### Step 2: Iteration $k = 1$ (Vertex 1 as Intermediate)
Apply the recurrence $D^{(1)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(0)}[i][j], \; D^{(0)}[i][1] + D^{(0)}[1][j])$ for every *(i, j)* pair.

**Row-by-row computation (showing the comparison):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(1)}[2][1] &= \min(8, \; 8 + 0) = 8 \\
D^{(1)}[2][3] &= \min(2, \; 8 + \infty) = 2 \\
D^{(1)}[2][4] &= \min(\infty, \; 8 + 7) = 15 \\
D^{(1)}[3][2] &= \min(\infty, \; 5 + 3) = 8 \\
D^{(1)}[4][2] &= \min(\infty, \; 2 + 3) = 5 \\
D^{(1)}[4][3] &= \min(\infty, \; 2 + \infty) = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Matrix $D^{(1)}$:**

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
8 & 0 & 2 & 15 \\
5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\
2 & 5 & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** Allowing vertex 1 as a stopover, we found shorter paths from 3 to 2 (via 1), 4 to 2 (via 1), and 2 to 4 (via 1).

---

### Step 3: Iteration $k = 2$ (Vertex 2 as Intermediate)
Apply $D^{(2)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(1)}[i][j], \; D^{(1)}[i][2] + D^{(1)}[2][j])$.

**Key updates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(2)}[1][4] &= \min(7, \; 3 + 15) = 7 \quad \text{(no change)} \\
D^{(2)}[3][4] &= \min(1, \; 8 + 15) = 1 \quad \text{(no change)} \\
D^{(2)}[4][1] &= \min(2, \; 5 + 8) = 2 \quad \text{(no change)} \\
D^{(2)}[4][3] &= \min(\infty, \; 5 + 2) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Matrix $D^{(2)}$:**

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
8 & 0 & 2 & 15 \\
5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\
2 & 5 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** Vertex 2 as a stopover created a new shorter path 4 → 2 → 3 with weight 7.

---

### Step 4: Iteration $k = 3$ (Vertex 3 as Intermediate)
Apply $D^{(3)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(2)}[i][j], \; D^{(2)}[i][3] + D^{(2)}[3][j])$.

**Key updates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(3)}[1][4] &= \min(7, \; \infty + 1) = 7 \\
D^{(3)}[1][3] &= \min(\infty, \; \infty + 0) = \infty \quad \text{(no improvement, since } D^{(2)}[1][3] = \infty) \\
D^{(3)}[2][1] &= \min(8, \; 2 + 5) = 7 \\
D^{(3)}[2][4] &= \min(15, \; 2 + 1) = 3 \\
D^{(3)}[4][1] &= \min(2, \; 7 + 5) = 2 \\
D^{(3)}[4][3] &= \min(7, \; 7 + 0) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Matrix $D^{(3)}$:**

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
7 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\
5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\
2 & 5 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** Through vertex 3, the path 2 → 3 → 4 gave a much shorter route (weight 3) compared to 15. Also, 1 → 3 is still unreachable directly or through {1,2}.

---

### Step 5: Iteration $k = 4$ (Vertex 4 as Intermediate)
Apply $D^{(4)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(3)}[i][j], \; D^{(3)}[i][4] + D^{(3)}[4][j])$.

**Key updates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(4)}[1][3] &= \min(\infty, \; 7 + \infty) = \infty \quad \text{(note: } D^{(3)}[4][3] = 7, \text{ so this becomes } 7 + 7 = 14) \\
&\text{Re-check: } D^{(3)}[1][4] = 7, \; D^{(3)}[4][3] = 7 \Rightarrow 7 + 7 = 14 \\
D^{(4)}[1][3] &= \min(\infty, 14) = 14 \\
D^{(4)}[2][1] &= \min(7, \; 3 + 2) = 5 \\
D^{(4)}[2][3] &= \min(2, \; 3 + 7) = 2 \\
D^{(4)}[2][4] &= \min(3, \; 3 + 0) = 3 \\
D^{(4)}[3][1] &= \min(5, \; 1 + 2) = 3 \\
D^{(4)}[3][2] &= \min(8, \; 1 + 5) = 6 \\
D^{(4)}[3][3] &= \min(0, \; 1 + 7) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Resulting Matrix $D^{(4)}$ (All-Pairs Shortest Paths):**

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 14 & 7 \\
5 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\
3 & 6 & 0 & 1 \\
2 & 5 & 7 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** With vertex 4 now allowed, path 1 → 4 → 3 (weight 14) becomes the shortest route from 1 to 3. Path 3 → 4 → 1 (weight 3) beats the direct edge (weight 5). Since all diagonal entries are **0** (none negative), **no negative cycle exists**.

### Summary Interpretation Table

| Pair $(i, j)$ | Shortest Distance | Optimal Path |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 → 3 | 14 | $1 \to 4 \to 3$ |
| 2 → 1 | 5 | $2 \to 4 \to 1$ |
| 2 → 4 | 3 | $2 \to 3 \to 4$ |
| 3 → 1 | 3 | $3 \to 4 \to 1$ |
| 3 → 2 | 6 | $3 \to 4 \to 1 \to 2$ |
| 4 → 3 | 7 | $4 \to 1 \to 4 \to 3$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Floyd-Warshall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Graph G with V vertices]) --> inputA[Read Weight Matrix W of order V x V]
    inputA --> initA[Initialize D0 matrix equal to W]
    initA --> initB[Set D i i = 0 and D i j = infinity where no edge exists]
    initB --> loopStart{k = 1 to V}
    loopStart --> innerI{i = 1 to V}
    innerI --> innerJ{j = 1 to V}
    innerJ --> check{Is D i k and D k j both finite?}
    check -- Yes --> relax[Compute newDist = D i k + D k j]
    check -- No --> skip[Keep current D i j unchanged]
    relax --> compare{newDist less than current D i j?}
    compare -- Yes --> update[Update D i j = newDist]
    compare -- No --> skip
    update --> innerJ
    skip --> innerJ
    innerJ --> innerI
    innerI --> loopStart
    loopStart --> negCheck{Any diagonal D i i less than 0?}
    negCheck -- Yes --> negCycle[Negative Cycle Detected]
    negCheck -- No --> output[Output final D matrix as all pairs shortest paths]
    negCycle --> endB([End: Algorithm reports negative cycle])
    output --> endB

    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class startA,endB terminal
    class inputA,initA,initB,relax,update,skip,output process
    class loopStart,innerI,innerJ,check,compare,negCheck,negCycle decision
```

### Conceptual State Transition Diagram (Matrix Evolution)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0[D superscript 0 equals W - Direct edges only] --> s1[D superscript 1 - Add vertex 1 as stopover]
    s1 --> s2[D superscript 2 - Add vertex 2 as stopover]
    s2 --> s3[D superscript 3 - Add vertex 3 as stopover]
    s3 --> s4[D superscript 4 equals D superscript V - Final all pairs shortest paths]

    classDef state fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    class s0,s1,s2,s3,s4 state
```

### Why This Architecture Works (Conceptual Block Mapping)

| Phase | Block | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input Layer** | Read Weight Matrix W | Captures direct edge weights |
| **Initialization Layer** | Set D⁰ = W | Establishes base case for DP |
| **DP Iteration Core** | Triple nested loop (k, i, j) | Systematically adds intermediates |
| **Relaxation Block** | Min comparison logic | Dynamically updates shortest estimates |
| **Diagnostic Layer** | Diagonal check for negative cycle | Ensures validity of result |
| **Output Layer** | Final matrix D^V | Provides all-pairs shortest paths |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** 
**State the recurrence relation used in the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. What does each term in the relation represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The recurrence relation of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm is:

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])
$$

- **$D^{(k)}[i][j]$** represents the shortest path from vertex *i* to vertex *j* allowing vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediate vertices. **(1 mark)**
- **$D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$** is the shortest path from *i* to *j* that does **NOT** use vertex *k* as an intermediate. **(1 mark)**
- **$D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$** is the shortest path from *i* to *j* that **DOES** pass through *k*, computed as the sum of the shortest *i → k* path and the shortest *k → j* path. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** 
**How does the Floyd-Warshall algorithm detect the presence of a negative cycle in a graph? Justify with the diagonal entries of the final distance matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- After the Floyd-Warshall algorithm terminates, we examine the diagonal entries of the final distance matrix **D^V**. **(1 mark)**
- For any vertex *i*, the value **D[i][i]** represents the length of the shortest **cycle** starting and ending at *i*. **(1 mark)**
- If **any** diagonal entry is found to be **negative** (i.e., $D[i][i] < 0$), it implies the existence of a **negative cycle** in the graph, since the algorithm could keep traversing this cycle to reduce path lengths indefinitely. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the All-Pairs Shortest Path problem. Explain why Floyd-Warshall uses dynamic programming. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find the all-pairs shortest paths for the graph with the following weight matrix. Show all intermediate matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, D^{(3)}, D^{(4)}$. **(7 Marks)**

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & \infty & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Dynamic Programming Justification (7 Marks):**

The **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem requires finding the shortest path distance between **every pair of vertices** in a weighted graph. **(2 marks)**

Floyd-Warshall uses dynamic programming because: **(5 marks)**
1. **Optimal Substructure:** The shortest path from *i* to *j* either passes through vertex *k* or it does not. This naturally decomposes the problem into smaller overlapping subproblems.
2. **Overlapping Subproblems:** The same sub-paths (*i → k* and *k → j*) are reused multiple times across different vertex pairs, making memoization or iterative DP highly efficient.
3. **State Definition:** The state $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ captures the shortest path allowing vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediates. Each state is built incrementally from the previous one, ensuring polynomial-time solvability.

**(b) Step-by-Step Floyd-Warshall Execution (7 Marks):**

**Initial Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & \infty & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k=1$** (using vertex 1 as intermediate): Row 1 and Column 1 are unchanged because no edges lead to or come from vertex 1 except $D[1][2]=5, D[1][4]=10$.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & \infty & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k=2$** (using vertex 2 as intermediate): New paths via 2 emerge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(2)}[1][3] &= \min(\infty, \; 5 + 3) = 8 \\
D^{(2)}[3][3] &= \min(0, \; \infty + 3) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k=3$** (using vertex 3 as intermediate):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(3)}[1][4] &= \min(10, \; 8 + 1) = 9 \\
D^{(3)}[2][4] &= \min(\infty, \; 3 + 1) = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 9 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & 4 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k=4$** (using vertex 4 as intermediate): No updates as vertex 4 has no outgoing edges except to itself.

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 8 & 9 \\
\infty & 0 & 3 & 4 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 1 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Final Result:** $D^{(4)}$ is the all-pairs shortest path matrix. The diagonal entries are all zero, so **no negative cycle** exists.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the Floyd-Warshall algorithm with Dijkstra's algorithm in terms of:
   (i) Type of problem solved
   (ii) Time complexity
   (iii) Edge weight restrictions
   (iv) Approach (paradigm used) **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the directed graph with the following adjacency matrix, compute the all-pairs shortest paths using the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. Also, identify the shortest path from vertex 1 to vertex 5. **(7 Marks)**

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 8 & \infty & -4 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & \infty & \infty \\
2 & \infty & -5 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Floyd-Warshall | Dijkstra's Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Problem Type** | All-Pairs Shortest Path **(2 marks)** | Single-Source Shortest Path **(2 marks)** |
| **(ii) Time Complexity** | $O(V^3)$ **(1 mark)** | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with min-heap, or $O(V^2)$ for array version |
| **(iii) Edge Weights** | Works with **negative edges** (not negative cycles) **(1 mark)** | **Does NOT** work with negative edges |
| **(iv) Approach** | **Dynamic Programming** **(1 mark)** | **Greedy Method** |

**(b) Floyd-Warshall Execution (7 Marks):**

**Initial $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 8 & \infty & -4 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & \infty & \infty \\
2 & \infty & -5 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k=1$:** New paths via vertex 1: $D[2][5] = \min(7, \infty + (-4)) = 7$ (no change). $D[4][1] = 2$ (already exists).

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 8 & \infty & -4 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & \infty & \infty \\
2 & 5 & -5 & 0 & -2 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> Note: $D[4][2] = \min(\infty, 2+3) = 5$, $D[4][5] = \min(\infty, 2+(-4)) = -2$.

**$k=2$:** New paths via vertex 2: $D[4][3] = \min(-5, 5+\infty) = -5$. $D[1][4] = \min(\infty, 3+1) = 4$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 8 & 4 & -4 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
2 & 5 & -5 & 0 & -2 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> Note: $D[3][4] = \min(\infty, 4+1) = 5$.

**$k=3$:** New paths via vertex 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[1][3] &= 8 \;(\text{unchanged}) \\
D[2][3] &= \infty \\
D[4][5] &= \min(-2, -5+\infty) = -2 \\
D[1][5] &= \min(-4, 8+\infty) = -4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 8 & 4 & -4 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
2 & 5 & -5 & 0 & -2 \\
\infty & \infty & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k=4$:** New paths via vertex 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[1][3] &= \min(8, 4+(-5)) = -1 \\
D[1][5] &= \min(-4, 4+(-2)) = 2 \\
D[2][5] &= \min(7, 1+(-2)) = -1 \\
D[5][1] &= \min(\infty, 6+2) = 8 \\
D[5][2] &= \min(\infty, 6+5) = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & -1 & 4 & 2 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & -1 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
2 & 5 & -5 & 0 & -2 \\
8 & 11 & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k=5$:** New paths via vertex 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[1][4] &= \min(4, 2+6) = 4 \\
D[2][4] &= \min(1, -1+6) = 1 \\
D[4][4] &= \min(0, -2+6) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D^{(5)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & -1 & 4 & 2 \\
\infty & 0 & \infty & 1 & -1 \\
\infty & 4 & 0 & 5 & \infty \\
2 & 5 & -5 & 0 & -2 \\
8 & 11 & \infty & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Diagonal check:** All zeros → **No negative cycle**.

> **Shortest path from 1 to 5:** From $D^{(5)}[1][5] = 2$, the path is $1 \to 4 \to 5$ with total weight $2 + 6 = 8$? Wait, let me re-verify: $D[1][5]$ improved to $2$ via path $1 \to 4 \to 5$ where $D[1][4] = 4$? That gives $4 + (-2) = 2$. So the path is $1 \to 4 \to 5$ with edges: $W[1][4]$ initially was $\infty$ but became $4$ via $1 \to 2 \to 4 = 3 + 1 = 4$, then $4 \to 5 = -2$. **Shortest path: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ with total cost $3 + 1 - 2 = 2$.** ✓

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the infinity handling:** Students often use a very large number (e.g., 9999) and forget to add it, leading to **false shorter paths** (e.g., $9999 + (-5000) = 4999$ which is treated as smaller than the actual minimum). Always use the symbolic **∞** and apply the rule that **∞ + anything = ∞** (or any + ∞ = ∞).
> 2. **Off-by-one in matrix indexing:** The KTU convention usually uses **1-based indexing** for vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, V\}$. Writing $D^{(0)}$ when asked for $D^{(1)}$ loses marks.
> 3. **Missing the negative cycle check:** Not all students remember to verify $D[i][i] \geq 0$ for all *i*. Examiners often allocate **1 full mark** for this.
> 4. **Incomplete final answer:** Always state explicitly: "The diagonal has all zeros, so no negative cycle exists" and "The matrix $D^{(V)}$ gives the all-pairs shortest paths."
> 5. **Confusing the intermediate vertex concept:** The vertex *k* is an **intermediate** stopover, **NOT** a source or destination. Students mistakenly treat *k* as either *i* or *j*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Floyd-Warshall is a **Dynamic Programming** algorithm for the **All-Pairs Shortest Path** problem in $O(V^3)$ time. **(High Priority)**
- **The Recurrence (MUST MEMORIZE):** 
$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$$
- **Initialization Rule:** $D^{(0)}[i][j] = W[i][j]$, with $W[i][i] = 0$ and $W[i][j] = \infty$ for missing edges.
- **Number of Iterations:** Exactly **V iterations** (one for each vertex as a potential intermediate).
- **Negative Cycle Detection:** Check diagonal; if **any** $D[i][i] < 0$, a negative cycle exists.
- **Edge Weight Tolerance:** Handles **negative edges** (unlike Dijkstra), but **fails on negative cycles**.
- **Space Optimization:** The algorithm can be implemented **in-place** using a single matrix (no need for separate $D^{(k)}$ matrices).
- **Path Reconstruction:** A separate **predecessor matrix $\Pi$** must be maintained to reconstruct the actual shortest path, not just the distances.
- **Transitive Closure Variant:** Replace min with logical **OR** ($\lor$) and addition with logical **AND** ($\land$) for Boolean reachability.
- **Real-World Analogy:** "Shortest flight between every pair of cities, gradually allowing stopovers."
- **KTU Exam Focus:** Questions typically ask for (1) the final all-pairs matrix $D^{(V)}$ and (2) the shortest path between two specific vertices with reconstruction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cut Set and Its Properties, Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT401 - Mathematics for Computer and Information Science 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cut Set and Its Properties, Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Cut Set

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected undirected graph. A **cut set** of $G$ is a set $S \subseteq E$ of edges such that:

1. The removal of all edges in $S$ from $G$ disconnects the graph (i.e., $G - S$ is disconnected), AND
2. No proper subset of $S$ has this property (i.e., $S$ is **minimal** with respect to disconnection).

Equivalently, a cut set partitions the vertex set $V$ into two non-empty disjoint subsets $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that $S$ consists of **all** edges with one endpoint in $V_1$ and the other in $V_2$. The set of all such edges is denoted by $(V_1, V_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A cut set is **always minimal** — removing one less edge from a cut set leaves the graph connected. This minimality is what distinguishes a cut set from an arbitrary disconnecting set.

The size (cardinality) of the smallest cut set of $G$ is called the **Edge Connectivity**, denoted $\lambda(G)$:

$$\lambda(G) = \min\{|S| : S \text{ is a cut set of } G\}$$

For a disconnected graph, $\lambda(G) = 0$. For a connected graph with at least one bridge, $\lambda(G) = 1$.

## 1.2 Special Cut Sets: Bridges and Cut Vertices

**Cut Edge (Bridge):** A single edge $e$ such that $\{e\}$ forms a cut set. This is a cut set of size 1.

**Cut Vertex (Articulation Point):** A vertex $v$ such that removing $v$ and all edges incident to $v$ increases the number of connected components. Formally, $G - v$ has more components than $G$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing Related Concepts:**
> - A **bridge** is a cut set of size **1 edge**.
> - A **cut vertex** is a vertex whose removal disconnects; the edges incident to it collectively form a related cut set.

## 1.3 Connectivity Measures

Three related numerical invariants measure how "robust" or "well-connected" a graph is:

| Invariant | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Vertex Connectivity | $\kappa(G)$ | Minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects $G$ (or reduces it to $K_1$) |
| Edge Connectivity | $\lambda(G)$ | Minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects $G$ |
| Minimum Degree | $\delta(G)$ | The smallest degree among all vertices of $G$ |

For the complete graph $K_n$:
$$\kappa(K_n) = \lambda(K_n) = \delta(K_n) = n - 1$$

A graph $G$ is called **$k$-connected** if $\kappa(G) \geq k$, and **$k$-edge-connected** if $\lambda(G) \geq k$.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The City Power Grid:**
> Imagine a city's electrical network where substations are **vertices** and power lines are **edges**. A **cut set** is the smallest set of power lines you would need to sabotage to split the city into two unpowered regions. The **edge connectivity** $\lambda$ tells a security planner the *minimum number of lines* that must be protected simultaneously. A **bridge** is a single power line that, if cut, blackouts a whole neighborhood — these are the most critical vulnerabilities. A **cut vertex** (substation) is one whose failure cascades into multiple isolated districts.
>
> Similarly, **vertex connectivity** $\kappa$ answers: *"How many substations must simultaneously fail to break the grid?"* The hierarchy $\kappa \leq \lambda \leq \delta$ says: the grid is never harder to break by removing substations than by cutting lines, which is never harder than finding the most weakly-connected substation.

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- **Network Reliability:** Designing fault-tolerant communication networks (internet backbones, telecom systems) requires maximizing $\kappa$ and $\lambda$.
- **VLSI Design:** Identifying cut vertices in circuit graphs helps locate single points of failure.
- **Social Network Analysis:** Connectivity measures quantify how easily information (or a disease) can be blocked.
- **Transportation Planning:** Cut sets in road networks represent bottleneck locations where investment yields the highest connectivity gain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Properties of Cut Sets

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected graph. The following are the **canonical properties** of cut sets that appear in KTU examinations:

### Property 1 — Minimality
A cut set is a *minimal* set of edges whose removal disconnects $G$. If $S$ is a cut set and $e \in S$, then $S - \{e\}$ is **not** a cut set (the graph $G - (S - \{e\})$ remains connected).

### Property 2 — Partition Correspondence
Every cut set $S$ corresponds to a unique partition $V = V_1 \cup V_2$ (with $V_1, V_2 \neq \emptyset$, $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$) such that:
$$S = \{uv \in E : u \in V_1 \text{ and } v \in V_2\}$$

Conversely, every partition of $V$ into two non-empty subsets defines such a set of "crossing" edges, though the resulting set is a cut set only when it is minimal.

### Property 3 — Union of Cut Sets
If $S_1$ and $S_2$ are cut sets of $G$, then $S_1 \cup S_2$ either:
- Is itself a cut set, OR
- Contains a cut set as a subset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (Cut Set Existence):** Every connected graph $G$ that is not a tree contains at least one cycle, and any edge of a cycle belongs to **no** cut set. Conversely, every bridge belongs to **every** cut set of $G$ that separates the two components it creates.

### Property 4 — Cut Sets in Trees
In a tree $T$ on $n$ vertices:
- **Every edge is a bridge**, hence every single edge is a cut set.
- $\lambda(T) = 1$ for any tree with $n \geq 2$.
- The number of distinct cut sets equals the number of edges, i.e., $n - 1$.

### Property 5 — Fundamental Cut Sets
Given a spanning tree $T$ of $G$ and an edge $e \notin T$, adding $e$ to $T$ creates exactly one cycle $C$. The **fundamental cut set** (or **cocycle**) corresponding to $e$ is the set of edges of $T$ that, together with $e$, form the cycle boundary — i.e., the tree edges whose removal disconnects the two "sides" defined by $C$.

For a connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and a spanning tree $T$:
- $T$ has $n - 1$ edges, all of which are bridges of $T$.
- The number of edges not in $T$ (chords) equals $m - (n-1)$.
- The number of **fundamental cut sets** equals $m - n + 1$ (the cyclomatic number).

## 2.2 Properties of Connectivity

### Whitney's Theorem (1932) — The Fundamental Connectivity Inequality

> [!NOTE]
> **Whitney's Theorem (Pillars of KTU Graph Theory):** For every connected graph $G$ that is not a complete graph:
> $$\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$
> For the complete graph $K_n$: $\kappa(K_n) = \lambda(K_n) = \delta(K_n) = n - 1$.

This is the single most-asked connectivity relationship in KTU examinations.

### Harary's Theorem
A graph $G$ is $k$-edge-connected if and only if every two vertices are joined by at least $k$ edge-disjoint paths (Menger's Theorem — edge form).

### Menger's Theorem (Vertex Form)
A graph $G$ is $k$-connected if and only if every pair of distinct vertices is joined by at least $k$ internally vertex-disjoint paths.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Statement |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Edge Connectivity | $\lambda(G) = \min\{\vert S \vert : S \text{ is a cut set}\}$ |
| 2 | Vertex Connectivity | $\kappa(G) = \min\{\vert S \vert : S \subseteq V,\ G - S \text{ disconnected or trivial}\}$ |
| 3 | Whitney's Inequality | $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$ |
| 4 | Complete Graph | $\kappa(K_n) = \lambda(K_n) = \delta(K_n) = n-1$ |
| 5 | Tree | For any tree $T_n$: $\kappa(T) = \lambda(T) = 1$, $\delta(T) = 1$ |
| 6 | Cycle $C_n$ | $\kappa(C_n) = \lambda(C_n) = 2$, $\delta(C_n) = 2$ |
| 7 | Path $P_n$ | $\kappa(P_n) = 1$, $\lambda(P_n) = 1$, $\delta(P_n) = 1$ |
| 8 | Cut Set Count (Tree) | Tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n-1$ cut sets (all singletons) |
| 9 | Fundamental Cut Sets | Equals $m - n + 1$ (cyclomatic number / first Betti number) |
| 10 | Cut Set Lower Bound | For $k$-connected $G$: minimum degree $\delta(G) \geq k$ |
| 11 | Disconnected Graph | $\kappa(G) = \lambda(G) = 0$ |
| 12 | Singleton Graph | $\kappa(K_1) = \lambda(K_1) = 0$ (no disconnection possible) |
| 13 | Edges in $k$-connected | $m \geq \frac{k \cdot n}{2}$ for $k$-connected $G$ |
| 14 | Max Edges for Connectivity | If $\kappa(G) = k$, then $m \geq kn/2$ (since $\delta \geq k$ implies $2m \geq kn$) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Telecom Backbone Design | Maximize $\lambda$ so no small set of cable cuts isolates regions |
| Computer Network Topology | Design $k$-connected LANs to survive $k-1$ router failures |
| Graph Database Sharding | Cut sets identify natural partition boundaries for distributed storage |
| Power Grid Engineering | $\kappa$ quantifies vulnerability to substation attacks |
| Parallel Computing | $\kappa$ bounds the number of processor failures a network can absorb |
| Transportation | Cut set size = minimum roads to close to disconnect two regions |

## 2.5 Logical Steps to Identify a Cut Set

To determine if a set $S \subseteq E$ is a cut set, follow this algorithmic logic (used as a graph-theoretic reasoning procedure, **not** as code):

1. **Verify Connectivity:** Confirm $G$ is connected (use BFS/DFS reasoning).
2. **Compute $G - S$:** Remove all edges in $S$ from $G$.
3. **Test Disconnection:** Check whether $G - S$ is disconnected (does it have $\geq 2$ components?).
4. **Test Minimality:** For every $e \in S$, verify that $G - (S - \{e\})$ is still connected. If any proper subset already disconnects, $S$ is not a cut set.
5. **Identify All Cut Sets:** For each partition $(V_1, V_2)$ of $V$, take all crossing edges; the minimal ones are cut sets. The minimum size is $\lambda(G)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Complete Cut Set Enumeration

**Problem:** Consider the graph $G$ with vertices $V = \{a, b, c, d\}$ and edge set $E = \{ab, ac, ad, bc, cd\}$. Find all cut sets and determine $\lambda(G)$, $\kappa(G)$, and $\delta(G)$.

### Step 1 — Compute Vertex Degrees

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(a) &= |\{ab, ac, ad\}| = 3 \\
\deg(b) &= |\{ab, bc\}| = 2 \\
\deg(c) &= |\{ac, bc, cd\}| = 3 \\
\deg(d) &= |\{ad, cd\}| = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\delta(G) = \min\{3, 2, 3, 2\} = 2$$

### Step 2 — Enumerate Partitions and Crossing Edge Sets

The bipartitions of $V$ are $\binom{4}{2}/2 = 3$ unordered pairs (since $\{V_1, V_2\}$ is equivalent to $\{V_2, V_1\}$):

**Partition 1:** $V_1 = \{a\}, V_2 = \{b, c, d\}$
$$S_1 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ab, ac, ad\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_1| = 3$$

**Partition 2:** $V_1 = \{b\}, V_2 = \{a, c, d\}$
$$S_2 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ab, bc\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_2| = 2$$

**Partition 3:** $V_1 = \{c\}, V_2 = \{a, b, d\}$
$$S_3 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ac, bc, cd\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_3| = 3$$

**Partition 4:** $V_1 = \{a, b\}, V_2 = \{c, d\}$
$$S_4 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ac, ad, bc, cd\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_4| = 4$$

**Partition 5:** $V_1 = \{a, c\}, V_2 = \{b, d\}$
$$S_5 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ab, ad, bc, cd\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_5| = 4$$

**Partition 6:** $V_1 = \{a, d\}, V_2 = \{b, c\}$
$$S_6 = (V_1, V_2) = \{ab, ac, cd\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad |S_6| = 3$$

(Note: I deliberately count $|V_1| = 1$ partitions because singleton cuts reveal vertex connectivity.)

### Step 3 — Identify Minimal Disconnecting Sets (Cut Sets)

A disconnecting set is a **cut set** iff removal disconnects $G$ and no proper subset does.

- $S_2 = \{ab, bc\}$: Removing $ab$ and $bc$ leaves $G$ as $a-c-d$ connected via $ac, ad, cd$. So $G - S_2$ is connected! Wait — recheck: edges remaining are $\{ac, ad, cd\}$. Graph is path $a-c-d$, which is connected. So $\{ab, bc\}$ is **not** a cut set.
- We need to verify each. The minimal ones are those where removing one fewer edge keeps the graph connected.

**Better method:** Identify **bridges** (cut sets of size 1) and **vertex cuts** (sets of vertices whose removal disconnects).

**Bridges in $G$?** No edge of $G$ is a bridge because $G$ is 2-edge-connected (every edge lies on a cycle): for instance, $ab$ lies on cycle $a-b-c-a$.

**Vertex Cuts:** 
- Remove $a$: remaining edges $\{bc, cd\}$, vertices $\{b, c, d\}$ — connected via $b-c-d$.
- Remove $b$: remaining edges $\{ac, ad, cd\}$, vertices $\{a, c, d\}$ — connected via $a-c-d$.
- Remove $c$: remaining edges $\{ab, ad\}$, vertices $\{a, b, d\}$ — connected via $a-b, a-d$.
- Remove $d$: remaining edges $\{ab, ac, bc\}$, vertices $\{a, b, c\}$ — complete $K_3$ — connected.
- Remove $\{a, b\}$: remaining vertex $c, d$ with edge $cd$ — connected (2 vertices, 1 edge).
- Remove $\{b, d\}$: remaining $a, c$ with edge $ac$ — connected.
- Remove $\{a, b, c\}$: only $d$ remains — trivially connected (single vertex).
- Remove $\{a, b, d\}$: only $c$ remains — trivially connected.
- Remove $\{b, c, d\}$: only $a$ remains — trivially connected.

**No set of 1 or 2 vertices disconnects $G$.** Hence $\kappa(G) \geq 3$.

**Remove $\{a, b\}$:** Connected (just $c-d$). Not a vertex cut.
**Remove $\{a, c\}$:** remaining vertex $\{b, d\}$ with edge $bc$... wait, $bc$ is incident to $c$, so removed. Only edge $cd$... no, $cd$ incident to $c$. After removing $a$ and $c$: edges incident to $a$ or $c$ are removed: $\{ab, ac, ad, bc, cd\}$ — all 5 edges gone! Remaining: nothing. Graph: 2 isolated vertices. Disconnected!

So $\{a, c\}$ is a vertex cut of size 2. Hence $\kappa(G) = 2$.

### Step 4 — Find $\lambda(G)$

We need the minimum cut set. Looking at edge partitions:
- Smallest partition crossing set: $S_2 = \{ab, bc\}$ with size 2. We confirmed it's not minimal (already tested). So we need to find a partition whose crossing set **is minimal disconnecting**.

Re-examine $S_2 = \{ab, bc\}$: Actually I need to recheck — $G - \{ab, bc\}$ has edges $\{ac, ad, cd\}$. Vertices $a, b, c, d$ all present. Edges: $a-c, a-d, c-d$. So $a, c, d$ are connected; $b$ is **isolated**. Hence $G - S_2$ is **disconnected**! My earlier analysis was wrong. So $S_2$ disconnects $G$.

Check minimality: $G - \{ab\}$: edges $\{ac, ad, bc, cd\}$. Graph: $a$ connected to $c, d$; $b$ connected to $c$; $c$ connected to $d$. So $a-b-c-d$ all connected. Not disconnected. $G - \{bc\}$: edges $\{ab, ac, ad, cd\}$. Graph: $a-b$ edge, $a-c$, $a-d$, $c-d$. All four vertices connected. Not disconnected. So neither single edge is a cut set, but $\{ab, bc\}$ together is. **$S_2$ is a cut set of size 2.**

**Therefore $\lambda(G) = 2$.**

### Step 5 — Verify Whitney's Inequality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa(G) = 2,\quad \lambda(G) = 2,\quad \delta(G) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Whitney says $\kappa \leq \lambda \leq \delta$:
$$2 \leq 2 \leq 2 \quad \checkmark$$

All three are equal to 2, indicating a tightly connected graph.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Path Graph $P_5$

**Problem:** For the path graph $P_5$ with vertices $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5$ and edges $v_1v_2, v_2v_3, v_3v_4, v_4v_5$, find all cut sets, $\kappa, \lambda, \delta$.

### Step 1 — Compute Degrees

$$
\begin{aligned}
\deg(v_1) = \deg(v_5) = 1, \quad \deg(v_2) = \deg(v_3) = \deg(v_4) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\delta(P_5) = 1$.

### Step 2 — Bridges

Every edge of a tree is a bridge, so each single edge is a cut set:
$$\{v_1v_2\},\ \{v_2v_3\},\ \{v_3v_4\},\ \{v_4v_5\}$$

There are exactly $4 = n - 1$ cut sets, each of size 1. So $\lambda(P_5) = 1$.

### Step 3 — Cut Vertices

Removing $v_2$ disconnects: left side $\{v_1\}$, right side $\{v_3, v_4, v_5\}$. Similarly $v_3, v_4$ are cut vertices. So $\kappa(P_5) = 1$.

### Step 4 — Verify Whitney

$$1 \leq 1 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Proof of Whitney's Theorem: $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$

### Part A — Prove $\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$

Let $v$ be a vertex of minimum degree $\delta(G)$. Let $N(v)$ be the neighborhood of $v$ and let $E_v$ be the set of edges incident to $v$, so $|E_v| = \delta(G)$.

Consider the partition $V_1 = \{v\}$ and $V_2 = V - \{v\}$. The set of crossing edges is exactly $E_v$. Therefore:

$$(V_1, V_2) = E_v \quad \text{with} \quad |E_v| = \delta(G)$$

By the cut set correspondence, the **minimum** cut set has size at most $|E_v|$:

$$\lambda(G) \leq \delta(G)$$

**Equality** holds when the edges around a minimum-degree vertex already form a minimal disconnecting set.

### Part B — Prove $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$

**Case 1: $\lambda(G) = 0$.** Then $G$ is disconnected, so $\kappa(G) = 0$. Hence $\kappa \leq \lambda$.

**Case 2: $\lambda(G) = 1$.** Then there is a bridge $e = uv$. Removing the single vertex $u$ (or $v$) disconnects the graph (the bridge $e$ is gone, and $v$ becomes isolated or disconnected from the rest). Hence $\kappa(G) \leq 1 = \lambda(G)$.

**Case 3: $\lambda(G) \geq 2$.** Let $S = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_{\lambda}\}$ be a minimum cut set, with $|S| = \lambda(G)$. Suppose $S$ has $k$ distinct endpoints (where $k \leq 2\lambda$ since each edge contributes at most 2 endpoints). 

By carefully choosing one endpoint from each edge of $S$ (a known combinatorial argument), we can find a vertex set $W$ of size at most $\lambda$ whose removal disconnects $G$. The construction: orient each edge in $S$ arbitrarily, and select for each $e_i$ either its head or tail. With a smart choice, we obtain at most $\lambda$ vertices whose removal eliminates all edges of $S$, hence disconnects $G$.

Thus $\kappa(G) \leq \lambda(G)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Connectivity of Common Graph Families

| Graph Family | $n$ | $\kappa(G)$ | $\lambda(G)$ | $\delta(G)$ | Cut Vertices? | Bridges? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Path $P_n$ | $n$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | Yes (internal vertices) | Yes (every edge) |
| Cycle $C_n$ | $n$ | $2$ | $2$ | $2$ | No | No |
| Complete $K_n$ | $n$ | $n-1$ | $n-1$ | $n-1$ | No | No |
| Tree $T_n$ | $n$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | Yes (if $n \geq 2$, non-trivial tree) | Yes (every edge) |
| Wheel $W_n$ | $n+1$ | $3$ | $3$ | $3$ | No (hub has degree $n$) | No |
| Petersen Graph | $10$ | $3$ | $3$ | $3$ | No | No |
| Bipartite $K_{m,n}$ | $m+n$ | $\min\{m,n\}$ | $\min\{m,n\}$ | $\min\{m,n\}$ | No | No |
| Cube Graph $Q_3$ | $8$ | $3$ | $3$ | $3$ | No | No |

## 3.5 Example: Wheel Graph $W_4$ (5 vertices)

**Vertices:** Center $c$, rim $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4$. Edges: $c-v_i$ for all $i$ (4 spokes), and rim cycle $v_1v_2, v_2v_3, v_3v_4, v_4v_1$.

### Compute Degrees

$$
\deg(c) = 4, \quad \deg(v_i) = 3 \text{ for all } i
$$

So $\delta(W_4) = 3$.

### Find $\lambda$

The smallest disconnecting edge set has size 3. For instance, remove the 3 spokes $c v_1, c v_2, c v_3$. Then $c$ is isolated, while $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4$ are still connected via the rim. Hence disconnected. No 2-edge removal disconnects (the rim cycle keeps things together, and the remaining spokes keep the center connected). So $\lambda(W_4) = 3$.

### Find $\kappa$

Removing any single vertex: removing a rim vertex $v_1$ still leaves $c$ connected to $v_2, v_3, v_4$, and the rim $v_2v_3v_4$ is a path. So connected. Removing $c$: rim is a cycle $C_4$, connected. Removing any 2 vertices: try removing $c$ and $v_1$. Remaining: $v_2, v_3, v_4$ with edges $v_2v_3, v_3v_4$ — connected. Remove $v_1, v_2$: Remaining $c, v_3, v_4$ with edges $c v_3, c v_4, v_3 v_4$ — connected (triangle). 

Try removing 3 vertices: $v_1, v_2, v_3$. Remaining: $c, v_4$ with edge $c v_4$ — connected. 

Try removing $c, v_1, v_2$. Remaining: $v_3, v_4$ with edge $v_3 v_4$ — connected. 

So $\kappa(W_4) \geq 3$. Combined with $\kappa \leq \lambda = 3$, we get $\kappa(W_4) = 3$.

### Verify Whitney

$$\kappa(W_4) = 3 \leq \lambda(W_4) = 3 \leq \delta(W_4) = 3 \quad \checkmark$$

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Connectivity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Graph G Connected] --> B{Cut Set Exists?}
    B -- No --> Z[lambda G = 0, G Disconnected]
    B -- Yes --> C{Minimum Cut Set Size}
    C --> D[Size 1: Bridge]
    C --> E[Size 2: 2 edge disconnect]
    C --> F[Size k: k edge disconnect]

    D --> G[lambda G = 1]
    E --> H[lambda G = 2]
    F --> I[lambda G = k]

    G --> J{Whitney Bound}
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[kappa G less than or equal to lambda G]
    K --> L[lambda G less than or equal to delta G]
    L --> M[Final Connectivity Profile]

    classDef startNode fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef warnNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A startNode
    class B,C,J decisionNode
    class D,E,F,G,H,I resultNode
    class K,L,M resultNode
    class Z warnNode
```

**Reading the flowchart:** The decision tree first asks whether any cut set exists (it does for all connected graphs, except $K_1$). Then it branches on the minimum size, ultimately feeding into the Whitney bound $\kappa \leq \lambda \leq \delta$.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Fundamental Cut Set Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STEP1[Step 1: Identify Spanning Tree T]
        T[Tree T with n minus 1 edges]
    end

    subgraph STEP2[Step 2: Add a Chord]
        CH[Pick chord e not in T]
    end

    subgraph STEP3[Step 3: Form Cycle]
        C[Cycle C = T union e minus edges on path]
    end

    subgraph STEP4[Step 4: Define Cut Set]
        S[Fundamental Cut Set S_e = edges of T that disconnect when e is removed]
    end

    T --> CH
    CH --> C
    C --> S

    classDef stepBox fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class T,CH,C,S stepBox
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical 4-step procedure to construct a **fundamental cut set** from a chord. It is the bridge between spanning trees, cycles, and cut sets — a frequent KTU exam question.

**Reading the diagram:** The architecture shows 3 internally vertex-disjoint paths from Source to Destination: Path A (R1→R3), Path B (R2→R3), and Path C (R1→R4→R3). By **Menger's Theorem**, this network is **3-connected** — at least 3 routers/links must fail simultaneously to disconnect the source from the destination. This is exactly the engineering utility of $\kappa(G)$: it bounds the failure tolerance of the network.
